proposals.title: Implicit Theories of Situational Strength: Development and Validation of the Situational Mindset Scale (SMS)
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on mindsets has robustly examined beliefs about personal attributes (e.g., intelligence) and specific domains like illness. However, people also hold implicit theories about the nature of situations themselves—whether they are fixed, constraining entities ('situational-fixed mindset') or malleable contexts that can be shaped ('situational-growth mindset'). This gap is critical because such situational mindsets may independently predict coping, persistence, and intervention efficacy beyond established personal mindsets, particularly in high-stakes contexts like chronic illness or organizational challenges.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individuals hold measurable implicit theories about situational strength (fixed vs. malleable) and that a stronger situational-growth mindset will predict more adaptive coping strategies, higher perceived behavioral control, and greater persistence in the face of obstructive environments, even after controlling for personal growth mindset.
proposals.method: We will develop and validate the Situational Mindset Scale (SMS) across four studies using pre-2026 psychometric methods. Study 1 will generate items (e.g., 'A difficult situation is something you can fundamentally change') and establish factor structure via exploratory factor analysis (EFA) with a diverse sample. Study 2 will confirm the factor structure with confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) and establish convergent/discriminant validity against measures of personal mindset, locus of control, and the Illness Mindset Inventory. Study 3 will test predictive validity by linking SMS scores to behavioral persistence on an impossible-turned-possible puzzle task within a rigged 'fixed' environment. Study 4 will examine the SMS in an applied longitudinal field study with patients managing a chronic illness, assessing how situational mindsets predict health behaviors and coping over six months.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first validated instrument to measure implicit theories of situations, extending Dweck's framework beyond the personal domain. It will establish situational mindset as a novel construct with predictive power for real-world outcomes, offering a new target for interventions aimed at improving resilience and adaptive behavior in constraining environments.
proposals.novelty: While the fixed/growth dichotomy is well-established for personal attributes, its application to perceptions of situational strength itself is novel. This moves beyond asking 'Can *I* change?' to ask 'Can *this situation* change?', a distinct and unexplored layer of belief that may critically shape behavior.

proposals.title: Mindset as a Dynamic State: A High-Density, Context-Sensitive Assessment of Implicit Theories in Academic and Health Domains
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dweck's own work, often treats mindsets as relatively stable individual traits measured at a single time point, predicting long-term outcomes. This static view may obscure critical nuance, as mindsets about intelligence, personality, or illness likely fluctuate with daily experiences, feedback, and environmental cues. Understanding this dynamism is crucial for designing more effective, context-aware interventions that can be delivered at optimal moments of malleability.
proposals.hypothesis: Individuals' implicit theories (growth vs. fixed mindsets) function as state-like variables that show significant short-term variability, and this variability is systematically predicted by specific contextual factors (e.g., recent success/failure, type of feedback, stress levels) rather than being random noise.
proposals.method: We will conduct an intensive longitudinal study using ecological momentary assessment (EMA). Participants (N=200 undergraduates and 150 patients with a chronic illness) will complete brief, domain-specific mindset measures via a smartphone app 3 times daily for 21 days. Concurrently, they will report on contextual variables (e.g., academic stress, health symptoms, received feedback). We will use multilevel vector autoregression (VAR) modeling to analyze within-person dynamics, treating mindset scores as time-series data to model lead-lag relationships and identify which contextual 'shocks' most reliably predict subsequent mindset shifts.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution empirical map of mindset variability, moving the field beyond trait-level correlations. It will identify the most potent 'mindset disruptors' in real-world settings, offering a new framework for timing interventions. The findings will challenge the interpretation of mindset as a fixed predictor and refine models of how beliefs interact with immediate experience.
proposals.novelty: While prior work acknowledges mindsets can be changed via interventions, it has not systematically investigated their natural, short-term fluctuations as a core property. Applying intensive longitudinal methods and time-series analysis from clinical psychology to the study of implicit theories represents a significant methodological and conceptual shift from a between-person to a within-person science of mindsets.

proposals.title: Mapping the Social Contagion of Growth Mindsets: A Network Analysis of Diffusion Across Socioeconomic Strata
proposals.motivation: Carol Dweck's pre-2026 work demonstrates that growth mindsets can be taught and improve outcomes in education and health. However, the mechanisms by which these mindsets spread organically through social networks, and whether such diffusion can bridge or widen socioeconomic gaps in opportunity, remain largely unknown. Understanding these peer-to-peer transmission pathways is critical for designing scalable, equitable interventions that leverage natural social systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that growth mindsets will diffuse through peer networks via social influence mechanisms, but that the rate and breadth of diffusion will be moderated by network structure and socioeconomic status (SES), with more constrained diffusion in lower-SES networks due to structural barriers.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal, multi-cohort study in secondary schools stratified by SES. Using pre-validated scales (e.g., IMI, implicit theories of intelligence), we will measure mindset beliefs at three time points over one academic year. Concurrently, we will map students' friendship and advice networks using sociometric surveys. Social network analysis (SNA) will be employed using stochastic actor-oriented models (SAOMs) in RSIena to statistically model the co-evolution of networks and mindsets, testing for social influence and selection effects while controlling for SES.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical model of growth mindset contagion within real-world social networks, identifying key influencers and structural bottlenecks. It will contribute directly to intervention science by revealing how to seed mindsets for maximal peer-driven spread and will offer critical insights into whether mindset interventions can become self-sustaining in diverse communities.
proposals.novelty: While Dweck's team has tested direct interventions, applying dynamic SNA to model the *diffusion* of mindsets as a social contagion process is a novel integration of sociological methods with core psychological theory. Explicitly testing how network topology and SES moderate this diffusion moves beyond individual-level analysis to a systems-level understanding.

proposals.title: Modeling the Emergence of Mindsets: A Computational Reinforcement Learning Account of How Feedback Histories Shape Implicit Beliefs
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Dweck and others has established that mindsets (e.g., fixed vs. growth) are powerful predictors of resilience and achievement. However, the precise mechanisms by which individuals' histories of success, failure, and feedback crystallize into these broad belief systems remain underspecified. A formal computational model would provide a mechanistic, testable theory of this developmental process, bridging social-cognitive psychology with computational psychiatry and learning sciences.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an individual's implicit theory (mindset) can be formalized as a learned prior over the expected efficacy of effort, which is shaped via reinforcement learning from a history of task outcomes and feedback. Specifically, a 'fixed' mindset will emerge from models that learn a low, stable prior on effort's value from volatile or punishing feedback.
proposals.method: We will develop and fit computational models to behavioral data from longitudinal mindset studies. Using a hierarchical Bayesian modeling framework, we will treat mindset as a latent parameter governing how agents update their beliefs about effort's utility. We will implement and compare model-free (e.g., Rescorla-Wagner) and model-based reinforcement learning algorithms to simulate how sequences of success/failure feedback shape this prior. Model comparison will use metrics like WAIC and posterior predictive checks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal computational account of mindset development, generating precise, falsifiable predictions about how feedback patterns lead to different belief systems. It will offer a new tool for designing personalized interventions by identifying critical 'tipping points' in feedback sequences that reinforce maladaptive mindsets.
proposals.novelty: While reinforcement learning models have been applied to belief updating in many domains, their application to the formation of broad, domain-general implicit theories (mindsets) is novel. This moves beyond correlational and intervention-based studies to propose a specific algorithmic mechanism for a core construct in social-cognitive psychology.

proposals.title: The Protective Role of Fixed Mindsets: A Dual-Function Investigation in Chronic Health Management
proposals.motivation: The dominant pre-2026 literature on mindsets, pioneered by Dweck, robustly demonstrates the advantages of a growth mindset for learning and resilience. However, this has created a potentially oversimplified 'good vs. bad' dichotomy, where fixed mindsets are uniformly maladaptive. This proposal challenges that binary by investigating whether, in specific high-stakes domains like chronic illness management, a fixed mindset about certain illness attributes (e.g., its chronicity) might serve adaptive functions, such as reducing futile striving stress or facilitating acceptance, thereby improving well-being.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in the context of managing a chronic, non-curable illness, holding a more fixed mindset regarding the immutable nature of the disease will be associated with lower stress from futile control efforts and greater psychological acceptance, which in turn predicts better adherence to symptom management routines and higher quality of life, compared to a growth mindset focused on 'beating' the disease.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal, mixed-methods study with 300 patients diagnosed with Type 2 diabetes or rheumatoid arthritis. Using the validated Illness Mindset Inventory (IMI), we will measure fixed vs. growth mindsets about the illness's permanence. We will employ ecological momentary assessment (EMA) via smartphone to measure daily stress, coping efforts, and adherence. Statistical analyses will use hierarchical linear modeling (HLM) to test for moderation and mediation, examining if a fixed mindset buffers the link between disease-related setbacks and distress, and if acceptance mediates the link between fixed mindset and regimen adherence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more nuanced, context-dependent theory of mindsets, moving beyond a universal prescription for growth mindsets. It will contribute to health psychology by identifying when and how certain beliefs can be protective, potentially informing more personalized clinical communications that validate patient experiences without undermining self-efficacy.
proposals.novelty: This directly challenges the implicit valorization of growth mindsets by empirically testing their potential downsides and the protective benefits of fixed mindsets in a real-world, high-stakes domain, an investigation that is largely absent from pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Training the Feedback Giver: A Scalable Intervention to Promote Process-Focused Feedback Among Adult Educators
proposals.motivation: Extensive pre-2026 research by Dweck and others demonstrates that process praise (focusing on effort, strategy) fosters a growth mindset and resilience, while person praise (focusing on innate traits) can undermine it. However, most interventions target the *recipient* of feedback (e.g., students, patients). A critical gap exists in systematically training *adults in authority roles* (e.g., teachers, managers, clinicians) to generate high-quality, process-focused feedback effectively, which is a scalable lever for creating growth-minded environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, theory-based intervention teaching the core components of process-focused feedback will significantly increase adults' ability to generate such feedback and, in turn, improve the motivational responses of feedback recipients compared to a control training.
proposals.method: We will develop and test a multi-session workshop intervention for adult educators. Using a randomized controlled trial design, participants will be assigned to the intervention or an active control (generic communication training). The intervention will employ established methods from Dweck's prior work: (1) teaching the fixed vs. growth mindset theory, (2) using video-based mastery modeling to demonstrate effective vs. ineffective feedback, (3) guided practice with scenario-based exercises (e.g., responding to a student's failed assignment), and (4) peer feedback on practice attempts. Primary outcomes will be coded quality of feedback in standardized written and video responses pre- and post-intervention, and secondary outcomes will assess the impact on a separate sample of students who receive feedback from trained vs. control participants.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a validated, scalable protocol for training process-focused feedback delivery, moving beyond recipient-focused mindset interventions. It will provide causal evidence on whether improving feedback *givers'* skills translates to better recipient outcomes, offering a practical tool for organizations and institutions aiming to cultivate growth-minded cultures.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies *what* feedback is effective, this proposal innovates by creating and testing a *how-to* training program for the feedback giver, shifting the intervention target from the mindset consumer to the mindset architect in professional and educational settings.

proposals.title: Testing the 'Cognitive Vaccine': A Randomized Controlled Trial of Growth Mindset as a Buffer Against the Negative Impact of Person-Focused Criticism in Children
proposals.motivation: Chronic exposure to negative person feedback (e.g., 'you're not smart') is a known social pathogen linked to maladaptive outcomes like helplessness and reduced self-worth. Pre-2026 research on mindsets shows their role in resilience, but a rigorous, causal test of a specific mindset as a prophylactic buffer against a standardized social stressor is lacking. This study directly addresses this gap by applying a preventive intervention trial logic from epidemiology to developmental psychology.
proposals.hypothesis: Children who receive a brief growth mindset of intelligence intervention (the 'cognitive vaccine') prior to exposure to negative person-focused criticism will show significantly less deterioration in state self-worth, persistence on a subsequent challenging task, and negative affect, compared to children in an active control condition.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pre-registered, double-blind randomized controlled trial with children aged 10-12. Participants will be randomly assigned to either a validated growth mindset of intelligence intervention or an active control (study skills) module. One week later, all children will participate in a standardized 'feedback challenge' where they receive predetermined, person-focused negative criticism on a task performance. Primary outcomes (state self-worth, persistence time on an impossible puzzle, and negative affect) will be measured immediately post-feedback. Analyses will use ANCOVA, controlling for baseline measures, to test the moderating (buffering) effect of intervention condition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, causal model for quantifying the protective efficacy of psychological constructs against social stressors, moving beyond correlation. It will extend Dweck's mindset theory into a preventive framework, demonstrating that a mindset can act as a cognitive immunization. The methodology offers a new template for rigorously testing other candidate 'protective factors' (e.g., self-compassion, effortful control) against specific adversities.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct methodological analogy to a vaccine efficacy trial, which is uncommon in social psychology, and in testing mindset as a *prophylactic* buffer against a controlled, ecologically valid social stressor rather than as a response to failure or challenge per se.

proposals.title: The Stability of Self-Concept as a Moderator: Testing the Persistence of Person-Feedback Effects in Adolescent and Adult Populations
proposals.motivation: Prior research by Dweck and others has robustly demonstrated that person-focused praise (e.g., 'You are smart') versus process-focused praise (e.g., 'You worked hard') can have divergent effects on children's resilience and motivation. However, this work has primarily focused on younger populations. It remains unknown whether these effects persist or even intensify in adolescents and adults, whose self-concepts are theorized to be more stable and resistant to change. Understanding this boundary condition is crucial for applying mindset principles effectively across the lifespan, particularly in adult education, workplace training, and clinical settings where feedback is constant.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the negative effects of person-focused feedback (e.g., on challenge-seeking and resilience after failure) will be more pronounced and persistent in adult populations compared to younger adolescents, due to adults' more crystallized self-theories. Conversely, we predict growth mindset interventions will require more intensive or tailored delivery to produce effects in adults.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-cohort, longitudinal experiment. Participants (N=600) will be stratified into three age groups: younger adolescents (13-15), older adolescents/young adults (18-25), and adults (35-50). In an online session, participants will complete a baseline assessment of implicit theories and self-concept clarity. They will then engage in a challenging problem-solving task and be randomly assigned to receive standardized person-praise, process-praise, or neutral feedback. Dependent measures, administered immediately, one week later, and one month later, will include subsequent task choice (challenge vs. easy), self-reported motivation, and attribution for failures. Analyses will use mixed-effects models to test for interactions between feedback type, age cohort, and time.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly test a key theoretical boundary condition of mindset theory—the role of self-concept stability—providing the first empirical evidence on how person-feedback effects evolve across development. It will offer practical guidance for tailoring feedback and mindset interventions for older populations in educational, organizational, and therapeutic contexts, moving the field beyond its school-age origins.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined age as a factor, no pre-2026 study has systematically compared the longitudinal durability of person-feedback effects from adolescence through middle adulthood, explicitly testing the moderating role of self-concept crystallization predicted by broader social-cognitive theory.

proposals.title: The Limits of Mindset: A Contextual Analysis of Growth Mindset Efficacy in the Presence of Severe Structural Barriers
proposals.motivation: Carol Dweck's pre-2026 research demonstrates the powerful effects of growth mindsets on motivation and achievement in education and health. However, critics argue that focusing on individual psychology may inadvertently overlook or minimize the role of systemic inequities. It is crucial to empirically test the boundary conditions of mindset theory to understand when psychological interventions are sufficient, and when they must be coupled with structural change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the positive effects of a growth mindset intervention on academic outcomes will be significantly attenuated, or rendered negligible, for students operating in contexts characterized by overwhelming structural barriers (e.g., severe resource deprivation, systemic discrimination), compared to students in contexts with moderate or low structural barriers.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-site, stratified randomized controlled trial. First, we will quantitatively assess structural barriers at the school/district level using pre-2026 indices (e.g., funding per student, student-to-counselor ratios, neighborhood opportunity metrics). Sites will be stratified into High, Medium, and Low-Barrier tiers. Within each tier, students will be randomly assigned to receive a validated growth mindset intervention (e.g., teaching that intelligence can be developed) or a control activity. Primary outcomes will be academic persistence, grades, and challenge-seeking behavior, measured over one academic year. We will use moderated regression analysis to test the interaction between intervention condition and barrier tier on outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, direct test of the contextual limits of mindset interventions, moving the field from a universalist perspective to a contingent one. It will offer concrete evidence for when and where mindset-based approaches are most effective, informing more ethical and targeted applications in policy and practice that consider both psychology and structure.
proposals.novelty: While past work has acknowledged context, it has not systematically manipulated mindset interventions across rigorously defined tiers of structural inequality to directly quantify the attenuation of effect sizes. This design explicitly tests the *interaction* between psychological and structural factors, a non-obvious but critical step for a mature science of intervention.

proposals.title: Beyond the Dichotomy: A Latent Profile Analysis of Implicit Theories in Academic and Health Domains
proposals.motivation: The dominant fixed-growth mindset model, while foundational, may oversimplify the structure of implicit theories. Pre-2026 research, including Dweck's own work on illness mindsets, suggests domain-specificity and potential for more complex configurations. A rigorous test of the binary assumption is crucial for refining theory and developing more precise, personalized interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implicit theories (e.g., about intelligence, personality, and illness) are not best represented by a strict binary but by a continuum or by multiple, distinct profiles that blend fixed and growth beliefs in domain-specific ways.
proposals.method: We will conduct large-scale, multi-domain surveys using established scales (e.g., Theories of Intelligence Scale, Illness Mindset Inventory). Data will be analyzed using Latent Profile Analysis (LPA) and Factor Mixture Modeling (FMM) to identify distinct subgroups. We will then validate these profiles by examining their differential predictive power for outcomes like challenge-seeking, resilience to academic setback, and health behavior adherence in longitudinal and experimental follow-ups.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a more nuanced empirical map of mindset structures, directly testing a core assumption of mindset theory. It will contribute to the field by potentially identifying new mindset profiles (e.g., 'cautious growth,' 'context-specific fixed') that could explain previously unaccounted-for variances in behavior and inform the next generation of targeted mindset interventions.
proposals.novelty: While the continuum idea has been discussed, this study employs advanced person-centered statistical techniques (LPA/FMM) on multi-domain data to empirically test for non-binary structures, moving beyond variable-centered correlations to identify potential qualitative subtypes of belief systems.

proposals.title: A Multi-Domain, Mechanistic Approach to Scalable Mindset Interventions: Testing a Core Beliefs and Contextualized Practice Protocol
proposals.motivation: Despite the promise of mindset interventions, meta-analyses (pre-2026) highlight concerns about effect size variability and replicability, especially in real-world settings. This limits their practical utility and scalability for improving outcomes in education and health. Developing more reliable intervention protocols is therefore a critical next step for the field.
proposals.hypothesis: A two-component intervention, targeting core beliefs about the malleability of personal attributes and providing contextualized practice in applying a growth mindset to domain-specific challenges, will produce more robust and replicable effects on behavioral outcomes than a standard belief-change-only intervention.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of randomized field experiments in diverse settings (e.g., high schools, community health clinics). The experimental protocol involves: 1) a standardized core module (online or in-person) teaching the neuroplasticity basis of growth mindsets, and 2) a contextualized practice module where participants identify and cognitively restructure fixed-mindset triggers specific to their context (e.g., a difficult math problem, a health setback). Effects will be measured on both self-report (e.g., IMI, implicit theories) and objective behavioral outcomes (e.g., course selection, treatment adherence) over 3-6 months.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a tested, mechanistic protocol for delivering more reliable mindset interventions across domains. It will advance theory by clarifying the active ingredients (belief change + application practice) necessary for durable effects. The resulting open-source protocol will enable rigorous, large-scale implementation and evaluation.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond testing isolated belief-change messages, this proposal integrates a core belief module with a novel, structured practice component tailored to specific field contexts, a combination not systematically evaluated in pre-2026 work.

proposals.title: Belonging Uncertainty as a Mediator of Math Anxiety and Avoidance in Early Adolescence: A Mindset Intervention Study
proposals.motivation: Math anxiety and avoidance emerge strongly in early adolescence, with long-term consequences for STEM participation. Pre-2026 research by Dweck and others shows that growth mindsets can improve academic outcomes and that belonging uncertainty is a key barrier for underrepresented groups. However, the specific role of belonging mechanisms—the belief that one does not or cannot belong in math contexts—in driving early adolescent math anxiety and subsequent avoidance remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that belonging uncertainty mediates the relationship between a fixed mindset about math ability and both math anxiety and behavioral avoidance. Further, we hypothesize that a synergistic intervention targeting both growth mindsets of ability and growth mindsets of belonging will reduce math anxiety and avoidance more effectively than an ability-mindset intervention alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal, randomized controlled trial with ~800 middle school students (Grades 6-7). Participants will be randomly assigned to one of three online, self-administered modules: 1) a synergistic growth mindset intervention (addressing both ability and belonging as malleable), 2) a standard growth mindset of ability intervention, or 3) an active control. We will use validated pre- and post-measures (and 3-month follow-up) of: math mindsets (adapted from Dweck's scales), belonging uncertainty in math (adapted from Walton & Cohen), math anxiety (using the MASC), and behavioral avoidance (via a choice task offering extra-credit math vs. verbal problems). Data will be analyzed using structural equation modeling (SEM) to test mediation and moderation effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct test of belonging uncertainty as a mechanism in early adolescent math anxiety, extending Dweck's mindset theory into the social-developmental domain. It will also offer practical insight into whether integrated mindset interventions are more potent than single-target interventions for preventing STEM disengagement at a critical developmental juncture.
proposals.novelty: While mindsets about illness and academic ability have been studied, applying a belonging mindset framework to math anxiety in early adolescence is novel. Testing a synergistic intervention that combines growth mindsets of ability and belonging to target both cognitive and social threats represents a significant advance over prior single-construct interventions.

proposals.title: Scaling a Growth Mindset and Belonging Intervention in MOOCs: A Randomized Controlled Trial to Reduce Math Anxiety and Increase Persistence
proposals.motivation: Massive Open Online Courses (MOOCs) offer unprecedented access to education but suffer from high dropout rates, particularly in challenging subjects like mathematics. Pre-2026 research indicates math anxiety and fixed mindsets about ability are significant barriers to persistence. This project addresses the critical need to embed scalable, evidence-based psychological interventions within digital learning environments to support diverse, global learners.
proposals.hypothesis: Embedding brief, strategically timed growth mindset and social belonging messages within the instructional flow of a math MOOC will reduce students' fixed-ability beliefs and math anxiety, leading to increased course persistence, engagement with challenging problems, and final achievement compared to a control condition.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pre-registered, double-blind randomized controlled trial on a major platform (e.g., Coursera or edX). Using the platform's A/B testing infrastructure, learners will be randomly assigned to receive either standard instruction (control) or instruction interleaved with intervention modules. These modules, developed from prior validated scripts, will include short videos and reflective exercises emphasizing that math ability can grow (growth mindset) and that struggles are normal and temporary (belonging). Key dependent variables, measured via embedded surveys and platform analytics, will include pre-post changes on the Implicit Theories of Intelligence Scale, math anxiety scales, clickstream data on problem attempts (especially after failures), and ultimate course completion.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first large-scale experimental evidence on the efficacy of integrating mindset and belonging interventions directly into MOOC content. It will advance the field of educational psychology by testing theory in a real-world, digital setting at scale and offer a concrete, exportable intervention model for online learning platforms seeking to improve student outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While mindset interventions have been tested in controlled K-12 and university settings, their integration and testing within the automated, asynchronous, and massive context of a MOOC—where instructor contact is minimal—is novel. It moves beyond one-time workshops to a 'nudging' model embedded in the learning architecture.

proposals.title: Cultivating a Systems-Growth Mindset: An Intervention to Increase Support for Structural Health Policies
proposals.motivation: Public health challenges like obesity and chronic disease are often framed in terms of individual responsibility (i-frame), which can lead to stigma and ineffective solutions. Structural or systemic (s-frame) policies (e.g., sugar taxes, urban planning) are often more effective but face public resistance. Pre-2026 research on mindsets shows they shape how people interpret challenges, but has not yet linked individual psychological traits to support for systemic change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a brief, targeted intervention fostering a 'systems-growth mindset'—the belief that societal systems and outcomes can be improved through collective effort and policy change—will increase support for and willingness to advocate for s-frame health policies, compared to a control intervention.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized controlled trial with a community sample (N=500). The intervention adapts Dweck's established growth mindset workshop format, using online modules with vignettes and reflective exercises to teach that health outcomes are malleable at a systemic level. The active control will receive an i-frame health education module. Primary outcomes, measured pre-, post-, and at 1-month follow-up, will be scores on a validated scale of support for s-frame policies and a behavioral measure (e.g., signing a petition for a local healthy food initiative). Analyses will use ANCOVA and mediation models to test effects and mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend mindset theory beyond individual achievement to the domain of civic engagement and policy support. It will provide the first experimental evidence that a scalable psychological intervention can increase public backing for structural solutions, offering a new tool for policymakers and public health advocates to build consensus for effective systemic change.
proposals.novelty: While mindsets have been applied to health behaviors (illness mindsets), applying them to beliefs about *systems* and linking them directly to policy preferences is a novel theoretical and applied leap. It bridges individual psychology with political and public health sociology.

proposals.title: The Dynamic Interplay of Identity Exploration, Mindset, and Competence Development: A Four-Year Longitudinal Study in Adolescence
proposals.motivation: Adolescence is a critical period for identity formation and the crystallization of academic and personal competence beliefs. Pre-2026 research by Dweck and others has established that mindsets (e.g., fixed vs. growth) powerfully influence motivation and resilience, but how these beliefs interact dynamically with the ongoing process of identity development is poorly understood. This gap limits our ability to design interventions that support holistic adolescent development, particularly in navigating challenges like academic setbacks or health issues where identity and competence perceptions collide.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adolescents who develop a more exploratory, 'growth-oriented' identity style (viewing the self as malleable) will be more likely to adopt and maintain growth mindsets about their abilities, leading to greater resilience in competence beliefs and sustained motivation over time, especially following perceived failures.
proposals.method: We will conduct a four-year longitudinal study following a diverse cohort of students from Grade 9 through Grade 12 (N=600). Using annual surveys, we will measure: 1) Identity styles (using adapted measures from Berzonsky's work), 2) Domain-specific mindsets (academic, social, illness via the validated Illness Mindset Inventory), and 3) Competence beliefs and intrinsic motivation. We will employ latent growth curve modeling (LGCM) and cross-lagged panel analyses to test directional relationships and co-development among these constructs. Specific moderators (e.g., socioeconomic status, critical health events) will be examined to identify boundary conditions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal map of how identity processes and mindset beliefs co-evolve to shape adolescent trajectories. It will extend mindset theory beyond specific domains (e.g., intelligence) into the core domain of the self. The findings will directly inform the development of more potent, identity-integrated interventions aimed at fostering adaptive development and preventing motivational decline during this sensitive life stage.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has linked mindsets to outcomes, this study uniquely positions identity exploration as a potential precursor and amplifier of growth mindsets, moving from a static 'belief' model to a dynamic 'self-system' model of adolescent development.

proposals.title: Social Transmission of Mindsets: A Longitudinal Study of Peer Influence on Academic Competence Beliefs in Secondary School Networks
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Dweck and others has established that students' mindsets about intelligence (fixed vs. growth) powerfully predict academic resilience and achievement. While social context is acknowledged as influential, the specific, granular processes by which peers transmit and shape these competence beliefs remain a 'black box.' Understanding these mechanisms is critical for designing interventions that can leverage positive peer dynamics and mitigate negative social comparisons in educational settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that peer influence on academic competence beliefs operates through two primary, measurable pathways: (1) the explicit verbal framing of challenges and failures (entity vs. process praise/criticism), and (2) the implicit behavioral modeling of effort and strategy use in response to academic setbacks.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal social network analysis study in 10 secondary schools (N≈1200 students). Students will complete the Implicit Theories of Intelligence Scale and a novel Peer Interaction in Learning Situations (PILS) survey at three time points. Using stochastic actor-oriented modeling (SAOM) with RSiena software, we will analyze co-evolution of friendship networks and mindset scores. Concurrently, we will use natural language processing (specifically, a pre-trained BERT model fine-tuned to classify entity vs. process language) to analyze transcripts from recorded peer study sessions, testing for linguistic contagion effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond correlational evidence to identify the specific, causal social mechanisms that alter mindset beliefs. It will provide a validated model of peer mindset transmission, offering concrete targets for interventions (e.g., training students in growth-minded communication). It will also extend Dweck's model of 'mindset climates' by empirically defining how such climates are created and sustained through micro-interactions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined classroom climates or teacher influences, this proposal uniquely integrates social network analysis and computational linguistics to dissect the real-time, peer-to-peer communicative and behavioral processes that underlie the social formation of academic self-concepts, a novel methodological synthesis in mindset research.

proposals.title: The Adaptive Fixed Mindset: Investigating Contexts Where Entity Beliefs Reduce Anxiety and Improve Performance
proposals.motivation: The dominant pre-2026 literature, including Dweck's foundational work, robustly demonstrates the benefits of a growth mindset for learning and resilience. However, this binary framing may oversimplify the psychological landscape. A nuanced investigation is critical to understand if and when a fixed mindset might serve an adaptive, self-protective function, particularly in high-stakes, performance-oriented contexts where perceived ability is under threat. This could refine mindset theory and inform more precise, context-sensitive interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: In specific, high-stakes evaluative contexts (e.g., major exams, competitive performances), individuals primed with a fixed mindset about a relevant ability will experience lower state anxiety and demonstrate better performance on the immediate task compared to those primed with a growth mindset, due to reduced cognitive load from effort justification and threat appraisal.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of randomized, controlled experiments with undergraduate students facing high-stakes midterm examinations. Participants will be randomly assigned to a brief priming intervention: a growth mindset article (control), a fixed mindset article, or a neutral article. Primary outcomes are pre-exam state anxiety (measured by the STAI-6) and actual exam performance. We will use mediation analysis (PROCESS Model 4) to test if anxiety mediates the mindset-performance relationship. A follow-up study will use a within-subjects design in a lab-based competitive task, measuring physiological stress (heart rate variability) and cognitive load (pupillometry) as mechanistic pathways.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first direct experimental evidence for potential adaptive benefits of a fixed mindset in circumscribed contexts, challenging the prevailing 'growth-is-always-better' heuristic. It will contribute a more complex, domain-contingent model to mindset theory and offer practical insights for tailoring motivational messaging to specific situational demands, rather than applying a universal growth mindset prescription.
proposals.novelty: This directly tests the non-obvious counter-hypothesis that the maladaptive label for a fixed mindset may not be universal, an idea underexplored in pre-2026 mindset research which primarily focuses on the benefits of growth mindsets. It innovatively applies the illness mindset framework (focusing on acceptance vs. change) to the domain of academic and performance ability.

proposals.title: Cultivating Organizational Resilience: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Growth Mindset Intervention for Corporate Leaders
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research demonstrates that a leader's mindset profoundly influences team performance and learning culture. However, most mindset interventions target students or educators, with limited application in high-stakes corporate leadership. In an era of rapid technological and economic disruption, fostering resilient organizations requires leaders who view challenges as opportunities for growth rather than threats to competence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a targeted, theory-based growth mindset intervention for CEOs and senior managers will lead to measurable increases in their growth-oriented leadership behaviors, which will, in turn, predict improvements in their teams' psychological safety, innovative problem-solving, and organizational resilience metrics.
proposals.method: We will adapt Dweck's established intervention protocols for an executive audience, creating a multi-module digital and workshop-based program. Using a waitlist-control randomized design, we will recruit 200 senior leaders from diverse industries. Primary measures include a validated Leadership Mindset Inventory (adapted from the Illness Mindset Inventory), 360-degree assessments of leadership behaviors, and organizational outcomes (e.g., team innovation rates, employee retention during crises). Analyses will employ multilevel modeling to test direct effects and mediation pathways.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first rigorous, experimental evidence for the causal impact of a growth mindset intervention at the executive level. It will extend mindset theory into the domain of organizational leadership and produce a validated, scalable training protocol for developing resilient leadership. The findings will bridge psychological science with leadership development practice.
proposals.novelty: While mindset interventions exist, applying them directly to CEOs and measuring downstream organizational resilience is novel. It moves beyond individual academic outcomes to test the theory's power to transform systemic organizational culture and adaptive capacity under stress.

proposals.title: Nudging Toward Growth: A Randomized Field Experiment Testing Choice Architecture to Promote Growth Mindset Behaviors in Academic Recovery
proposals.motivation: Mindset interventions, while effective, often require explicit instruction and can be resource-intensive to deliver at scale. Behavioral economics, particularly nudge theory (Thaler & Sunstein, 2008), offers a complementary approach by subtly altering the choice environment to steer behavior automatically. Integrating these fields could create low-cost, scalable methods to reinforce growth mindset principles, especially for students at risk of academic disengagement, where timely, subtle prompts may be more effective than formal lessons.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that embedding growth-mindset-aligned nudges into the digital learning environment of students on academic probation will lead to increased engagement with academic support resources, more frequent use of adaptive learning strategies (e.g., re-taking quizzes, viewing feedback), and greater improvement in subsequent course grades, compared to a control group.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized controlled trial with undergraduate students on academic probation. The intervention will implement three digitally delivered nudges within the university's Learning Management System (LMS): 1) a default option setting that pre-selects 'Review feedback and retry' after a failed quiz, 2) a framing nudge where messages about academic support services emphasize 'building skills' (growth) versus 'fixing problems' (fixed), and 3) a social norm nudge displaying anonymized data on how many peers accessed extra practice materials. Engagement metrics (clicks, time spent) will be logged by the LMS, and end-of-semester grades will be collected. Analyses will use linear and logistic regression models to test for between-group differences.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental evidence for the efficacy of choice architecture in promoting growth mindset behaviors outside of explicit teaching paradigms. It will contribute a scalable, low-touch intervention model that educational institutions can adapt. Furthermore, it will extend Dweck's mindset theory into the domain of behavioral economics, demonstrating how environmental cues can automatically channel behavior toward growth-oriented pathways.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has tested mindset interventions and nudges separately, this proposal uniquely integrates them by designing nudges whose architecture is directly derived from the core tenets of growth mindset theory (e.g., valuing effort and learning from setbacks). It moves beyond telling people about a growth mindset to architecting environments that make growth-minded choices the easier, default path.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Computational Model of Mindset: Formalizing Belief Updating about Self-Ability in Response to Feedback
proposals.motivation: Dweck's pre-2026 research established that mindsets (growth vs. fixed) are foundational beliefs about the malleability of personal attributes, influencing motivation and resilience. However, the precise cognitive mechanisms through which these beliefs update in response to success or failure remain underspecified. A formal computational model is needed to move from descriptive theory to a mechanistic account of how mindsets shape the interpretation of new evidence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an individual's mindset can be formalized as a prior belief distribution over their own ability, which systematically biases the Bayesian updating process when integrating performance feedback. Specifically, a fixed mindset will act as a strong, narrow prior, leading to attenuated belief updating after failure, while a growth mindset will act as a weaker, more diffuse prior, facilitating belief updating and learning.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical Bayesian model where participants' trial-by-trial estimates of their own ability are the observed data. The model will treat mindset (measured via the Implicit Theories of Intelligence Scale) as modulating the precision (inverse variance) of the prior distribution over ability. We will fit this model to behavioral data from a novel task where participants receive manipulated performance feedback on a challenging cognitive test. Model comparison (e.g., using WAIC or Bayes factors) will test if the mindset-modulated model outperforms a null model with a fixed prior.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal computational account of the mindset construct, specifying its role in belief updating. It will generate precise, testable predictions about how mindset influences learning trajectories and resilience to setbacks, bridging social-cognitive psychology and computational psychiatry. The resulting Illness Mindset Inventory (IMI)-informed model could later be adapted for clinical health contexts.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian models of learning are established, applying them to formalize the social-cognitive construct of 'mindset' as a prior is novel. This moves beyond correlational studies to a mechanistic model that can simulate intervention effects, such as how a mindset intervention alters prior precision and subsequent learning.

proposals.title: Systemic Scaffolds of Fixed Beliefs: A Multi-Method Investigation of Institutional and Cultural Reinforcers of Fixed Mindsets
proposals.motivation: While decades of research have focused on changing individual mindsets, the systemic origins of fixed mindsets remain largely unexamined. Understanding the environmental and structural factors that create and sustain fixed-mindset cultures is critical for moving beyond individual-level interventions to achieve scalable, lasting change in education, organizations, and healthcare. This addresses a core gap in the pre-2026 literature, which has primarily treated mindsets as individual psychological attributes rather than as outcomes of systemic design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, identifiable features of institutional systems—such as high-stakes, norm-referenced evaluation structures, binary success/failure feedback, and fixed-ability narratives in cultural messaging—systematically predict the prevalence of fixed mindset beliefs within a population, independent of individual differences.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-method, multi-site design. First, a large-scale correlational study will survey students, employees, and patients (N~3000) using adapted versions of validated mindset scales (e.g., IMI) and novel questionnaires quantifying perceived systemic incentives. Second, we will conduct a linguistic analysis of institutional policy documents and public-facing communications from these sites using pre-2026 natural language processing techniques (e.g., LIWC dictionaries, topic modeling) to identify language associated with fixed-ability constructs. Third, an experimental vignette study will test the causal impact of exposure to different systemic rules (e.g., 'rank-order grading' vs. 'mastery-based feedback') on temporary mindset states.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first integrated framework for identifying 'fixed-mindset systems,' shifting the field's focus from fixing the individual to diagnosing and redesigning contexts. It will yield a validated instrument for auditing systemic mindset cues and offer evidence-based guidelines for creating structures that naturally foster growth mindsets, thereby enhancing the impact and sustainability of mindset interventions.
proposals.novelty: This proposal inverts the classic mindset intervention paradigm by treating the context as the primary unit of analysis and the mindset as the outcome, rather than the reverse. It synthesizes Dweck's psychological framework with methods from organizational sociology, policy analysis, and computational linguistics to answer the previously unaddressed 'inverse problem' of mindset formation.

proposals.title: Generalizability of Growth Mindset Interventions: A Multi-Site Randomized Trial Across Diverse School Contexts
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has robustly demonstrated the benefits of growth mindset interventions for student motivation and achievement, but these findings are often based on studies in relatively homogeneous or limited school settings. To ensure these interventions are equitable and effective at scale, it is critical to test their efficacy and implementation fidelity across a wider range of demographic, socioeconomic, and geographic contexts. This study addresses a key gap in understanding for whom and under what conditions these interventions work best.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a standardized growth mindset intervention will show positive effects on academic engagement and grades across diverse school contexts, but that the magnitude of these effects will be moderated by school-level factors such as socioeconomic disadvantage, prior achievement levels, and school climate.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pre-registered, school-level cluster randomized controlled trial in 60 public high schools, stratified to ensure representation across urban, suburban, and rural locales, and varying in student racial/ethnic composition and free/reduced lunch rates. Schools will be randomly assigned to receive a validated online growth mindset intervention (teaching that intellectual abilities can be developed) or a control activity. Primary outcomes will be core course grades and student-reported challenge-seeking, measured at baseline and at the end of the academic year. We will use multilevel linear modeling (HLM) to analyze nested data, testing for main effects and cross-level interactions between intervention condition and school-level moderators.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the most comprehensive evidence to date on the generalizability of growth mindset interventions, directly informing scalable educational policy. It will identify contextual factors that enhance or diminish intervention effectiveness, offering practical guidance for tailoring implementation. The findings will advance the field beyond efficacy testing to a more nuanced understanding of real-world effectiveness.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has tested mindset interventions in diverse *individuals*, this study systematically manipulates and tests the intervention across diverse *institutional contexts* as a key experimental factor. It moves beyond asking if the intervention works on average to rigorously investigating the boundary conditions of its effectiveness.

proposals.title: Unpacking the Black Box: Testing the Mediating Role of Challenge-Seeking and Help-Seeking in a Growth Mindset Intervention for At-Risk Students
proposals.motivation: While growth mindset interventions have shown promise in improving academic outcomes for at-risk students, the specific mechanisms of change remain a 'black box.' Understanding the precise psychological and behavioral pathways is critical for refining interventions to maximize their efficacy and scalability. This research directly addresses a key gap in the pre-2026 literature by moving beyond demonstrating *that* interventions work to explaining *how* they work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a growth mindset intervention improves grades for at-risk students primarily by increasing two key behaviors: (1) the propensity to seek out challenging academic opportunities, and (2) the willingness to seek help when facing difficulties. We further hypothesize that these effects will be moderated by students' initial levels of belonging uncertainty and perceived teacher support.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized controlled trial with 800 at-risk high school students (e.g., low prior achievement, first-generation status). The intervention group will receive a validated, web-based growth mindset module. We will use multi-wave longitudinal data collection to measure mindset beliefs, challenge-seeking intentions (via hypothetical scenario measures), and actual help-seeking behaviors (via self-report and institutional data on tutoring center use). We will employ structural equation modeling (SEM) with bootstrapping to test for mediation and use moderated mediation analysis (PROCESS macro) to examine the proposed moderators of belonging and teacher support.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a granular, process-oriented model of how a growth mindset translates into academic improvement, identifying the most potent active ingredients. It will offer practical insights for educators by highlighting which supportive conditions (e.g., teacher support) are most crucial for intervention success. The findings will advance theory by integrating mindset constructs with behavioral and contextual moderators.
proposals.novelty: This proposal moves beyond testing mindset change as a monolithic mediator to dissect it into specific, measurable behavioral pathways (challenge- and help-seeking). Furthermore, it uniquely integrates the moderating role of the social-psychological climate (belonging, teacher support), testing a more complex, context-sensitive model of intervention effects than prior work.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of an Adaptive Learning Platform for Delivering and Testing Growth Mindset Interventions in Real-Time
proposals.motivation: While growth mindset interventions show promise, their effects can be heterogeneous and fade over time. Pre-2026 educational technology (ed-tech) platforms collect rich, real-time performance data but typically use it only for content sequencing, not for psychological support. This project addresses the critical need to make mindset interventions more dynamic, personalized, and responsive to students' ongoing learning experiences to sustain their impact.
proposals.hypothesis: An adaptive learning platform that delivers tailored growth mindset content based on a student's real-time performance patterns (e.g., struggle, recovery, plateau) will produce stronger and more durable improvements in students' mindsets, engagement, and academic outcomes than a static, one-time mindset intervention.
proposals.method: We will develop a plugin for existing adaptive learning platforms (e.g., using the xAPI standard) that monitors student performance data streams (quiz scores, time on task, error patterns). A rules-based algorithm, informed by pre-2026 mindset theory, will trigger specific micro-interventions (e.g., videos on 'learning from struggle' after repeated errors, messages on 'the power of yet' after a near-miss). In a randomized controlled trial, college students in a STEM course will be assigned to the adaptive mindset platform, a static mindset module, or a control condition, with outcomes tracked via the IMI, platform engagement metrics, and final grades.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel, scalable methodology for dynamically delivering psychological interventions. It will advance theory by testing how mindset beliefs are shaped and reinforced by specific, real-time academic events, moving beyond broad correlational claims to a fine-grained, mechanistic understanding.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel because it moves mindset interventions from a static, 'one-size-fits-all' workshop model to a dynamic, just-in-time support system embedded within the learning process, using educational data not for content adaptation alone but for psychological state inference and support.

proposals.title: Scaling Growth Mindset Interventions: A Randomized Trial of Teacher Training in Mindset-Supportive Feedback
proposals.motivation: Direct-to-student growth mindset interventions show promise but face scalability and sustainability challenges. Training teachers to embed mindset-supportive language into daily instruction offers a potentially more efficient and enduring pathway to change classroom culture. This approach aligns with Dweck's recent work on illness mindsets, which highlights the power of environmental messaging to shape core beliefs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a professional development program designed to train teachers in delivering growth mindset-supportive process feedback will lead to (a) significant increases in teachers' use of such language and (b) subsequent improvements in their students' growth mindsets, academic engagement, and mathematics grades, compared to a control group.
proposals.method: We will conduct a school-level cluster randomized controlled trial with 40 middle schools (20 intervention, 20 waitlist control). Intervention teachers will receive a 3-session workshop plus bi-weekly coaching, grounded in the established 'process praise' framework, to replace person-focused feedback (e.g., 'You're smart') with effort- and strategy-focused feedback (e.g., 'Your strategy of...'). Primary outcomes will be measured via: (1) coded audio recordings of teacher-student interactions, (2) pre/post student surveys assessing mindset and challenge-seeking, and (3) administrative math grades. Analyses will use multilevel modeling to account for nesting.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first rigorous, large-scale evidence on the efficacy of a teacher-focused mindset intervention as a scaling mechanism. It will contribute a validated, manualized teacher training protocol to the educational toolkit. Findings will advance theory by testing whether shifting the 'messenger' (teacher) is as or more effective than targeting the 'receiver' (student) directly.
proposals.novelty: While prior research has trained parents or tested brief student workshops, systematically training teachers to be consistent 'carriers' of mindset messaging in their daily practice is a novel and scalable implementation strategy. It shifts the intervention target from the student's belief system to the teacher's communicative behavior as a sustainable lever for change.

proposals.title: Beyond Universal Praise: A Multi-Cultural Field Trial Testing the Ecological Moderators of Effort vs. Intelligence Praise on Student Motivation
proposals.motivation: The dominant finding that effort praise fosters a growth mindset and superior motivation compared to intelligence praise is based largely on Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, and Democratic (WEIRD) samples. As mindset interventions scale globally, there is a critical need to test their boundary conditions. Pre-2026 cross-cultural psychology suggests values like individualism-collectivism and attitudes toward effort may moderate such effects, but large-scale, ecologically valid tests are lacking, risking the misapplication of a potentially non-universal intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: The motivational impact of effort praise versus intelligence praise is systematically moderated by pre-existing, culturally shaped value dimensions (e.g., independence-interdependence, incremental-entity theories of effort), such that effort praise will not be universally optimal and may be ineffective or detrimental in specific cultural configurations.
proposals.method: We will conduct a large-scale, pre-registered field experiment across 40+ diverse cultural sites (classrooms) worldwide. Using a standardized protocol, teachers will deliver scripted effort or intelligence praise following a controlled student task. Pre-intervention, we will measure cultural-level moderators via established scales (e.g., Triandis's individualism-collectivism, Lu et al.'s Implicit Theory of Effort Scale) and individual-level covariates. The primary outcome is behavioral persistence on a subsequent challenging task. Analysis will use multilevel modeling to test cross-level interactions between praise type and cultural/individual value dimensions.
proposals.contribution: This research will move the field from a universal to a contingent model of praise effects, generating an empirically derived 'map' of cultural moderators. It will provide practical, evidence-based guidance for tailoring mindset-aligned feedback in diverse global educational contexts. The ecological validity framework and multi-site field trial methodology offer a new paradigm for testing the generalizability of other psychological interventions.
proposals.novelty: It directly challenges a core, seemingly universal finding in mindset theory by applying an agronomy-inspired ecological validity framework, moving beyond simplistic East-West dichotomies to test specific, measurable cultural value dimensions as moderators in real-world settings.

proposals.title: Inoculating Motivation: A Randomized Trial Testing a Behavioral Vaccine Model of Effort Praise for At-Risk Students
proposals.motivation: Students at risk of academic disengagement often exhibit fixed mindsets, interpreting setbacks as evidence of limited ability, which undermines resilience. Pre-2026 research on stress inoculation and growth mindset interventions shows promise, but a precise, mechanistic model for proactively 'immunizing' motivation against failure is lacking. This gap is critical, as preventing motivational collapse is more efficient than remediating it after significant academic failure.
proposals.hypothesis: That a structured intervention delivering controlled, small-scale failures paired exclusively with process-focused effort praise will function as a cognitive-behavioral vaccine, strengthening associative pathways such that students later exhibit greater persistence, self-efficacy, and growth mindset beliefs when facing substantial academic challenges.
proposals.method: We will conduct a school-based randomized controlled trial with at-risk middle school students (N=300). The intervention group will complete a series of low-stakes, challenging puzzle tasks designed to induce short-term failure, administered via tablet. An adaptive algorithm will calibrate difficulty to ensure a ~70% failure rate. Immediately upon task completion, the software will deliver standardized, audio-visual effort praise (e.g., "I see how hard you worked on that strategy"). Control groups will receive either success praise or no praise after identical tasks. Pre-, post-, and follow-up measures will include the Implicit Theories of Intelligence Scale, a challenge-seeking behavioral task, and self-efficacy surveys, analyzed using mixed-effects modeling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanistic framework for understanding how motivational resilience can be proactively built, moving beyond broad mindset messaging to a targeted, dose-response model. It will yield a scalable, technology-assisted protocol for preventive intervention in educational settings, directly extending Dweck's illness mindset work into academic prophylaxis.
proposals.novelty: While growth mindset interventions are established, this proposal uniquely operationalizes the medical inoculation metaphor as a testable behavioral vaccine, using calibrated failure doses and contingent effort praise as the active 'antigen' to stimulate a protective cognitive immune response.

proposals.title: Shaping a Culture of Growth: A Cluster-Randomized Trial of a School-Wide Process Praise Intervention on Student Mindsets and Academic Resilience
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Dweck and others demonstrates that teacher praise focused on student effort and strategy (process praise) fosters growth mindsets and resilience more effectively than praise for intelligence. However, most interventions target individual teachers or classrooms, limiting their systemic impact. A school-wide cultural shift in praise practices could create a more consistent, reinforcing environment for student development, addressing a critical gap in scalable educational mindset interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a school-wide professional development program designed to train all teaching staff in the consistent use of process praise will lead to a stronger school-level growth mindset culture, which in turn will predict greater increases in students' academic perseverance and achievement, compared to control schools receiving standard professional development.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-year, cluster-randomized controlled trial in 30 middle schools. The intervention involves a multi-component program: 1) a foundational workshop on growth mindset and process praise theory, 2) a semester-long, structured peer-coaching protocol using video analysis of classroom interactions (coded with a pre-2026 validated scheme for praise type), and 3) monthly faculty meetings to share experiences and reinforce norms. Primary outcomes include student-reported growth mindset (Theories of Intelligence Scale), teacher-reported school culture of process praise (adapted IMI framework), and administrative math/reading standardized test scores. Analyses will use multilevel modeling to account for nested data.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first rigorous, large-scale evidence for a systemic, school-wide approach to fostering growth mindsets through praise culture. It will extend Dweck's work beyond individual-level interventions to demonstrate how organizational practices can be shifted to support student development. The findings will offer a concrete, replicable model for schools aiming to build a pervasive culture of growth.
proposals.novelty: While prior work tests mindset interventions on students or individual teachers, this proposal uniquely targets the entire school faculty as the unit of change, aiming to transform the macro-scale social environment. It innovatively applies the Illness Mindset Inventory's framework for assessing shared beliefs to measure school-wide praise culture, a novel construct in educational psychology.

proposals.title: Broadening the Scope of Growth: A Randomized Controlled Trial of an Incremental Theory Intervention in Secondary School Science and Language Arts
proposals.motivation: While Dweck's growth mindset interventions have shown success in mathematics, their generalizability to other core academic domains with distinct epistemic cultures and student perceptions remains underexplored. Pre-2026 research indicates that students often hold fixed beliefs about innate talent in areas like writing or scientific reasoning, which can create unique motivational barriers. Testing the intervention's efficacy in science and language arts is crucial for developing a comprehensive, domain-sensitive theory of academic mindsets and for creating scalable, subject-specific educational tools.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a tailored incremental theory (growth mindset) intervention will lead to stronger endorsement of growth mindsets, greater academic resilience, and improved end-of-term grades in secondary school science and language arts courses, compared to a control activity. We further hypothesize that the intervention's effects may be moderated by the specific subject domain and students' initial mindset beliefs.
proposals.method: We will conduct a school-based, pre-registered randomized controlled trial with 9th-grade students (N=600) enrolled in required science and English courses. Participants will be randomly assigned within classrooms to either a 45-minute online growth mindset intervention, adapted from Yeager et al. (2016) to include subject-specific narratives (e.g., struggling scientists, writers revising drafts), or an active control module on brain function. Primary outcomes include pre/post changes on the Implicit Theories of Intelligence Scale (adapted for domain-specific ability), behavioral measures of challenge-seeking and resilience via a follow-up task choice, and administrative end-of-term grades. Analyses will use linear mixed-effects models with random intercepts for classroom and student, testing for main effects of condition and interaction effects with subject domain.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, causal evidence on whether a brief, scalable mindset intervention can be effectively generalized beyond mathematics, directly informing curriculum design and teacher training. It will contribute to mindset theory by examining potential boundary conditions and domain-specific mechanisms, moving the field from a general intelligence mindset model toward a more nuanced understanding of how malleability beliefs operate across different learning contexts.
proposals.novelty: This work moves beyond the established literature on math and general intelligence mindsets to systematically test and compare intervention effects in two distinct, high-stakes academic domains (science and language arts) within the same experimental design, allowing for direct comparison of efficacy. The adaptation of intervention materials to address domain-specific stereotypes (e.g., the 'born writer' or 'science person' myth) represents a targeted, theory-driven innovation.

proposals.title: Sustained Effects of a Growth-Theory-of-Interest Intervention: A Four-Year Longitudinal Follow-Up into High School
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Dweck and others demonstrates that targeted mindset interventions can improve academic outcomes in the short term. However, a critical, unanswered question is whether these gains persist as students face new academic and social challenges, such as the transition to high school. Determining the long-term durability of intervention effects is essential for validating the practical utility and scalability of mindset-based approaches in education.
proposals.hypothesis: Students who received a growth-theory-of-interest intervention in middle school will demonstrate sustained, higher levels of academic interest and achievement in math and science during high school, compared to a control group, and this effect will be mediated by the maintenance of a growth mindset and stronger domain-specific beliefs.
proposals.method: This study will conduct a four-year longitudinal follow-up of the experimental and control cohorts from the original growth-theory-of-interest intervention study (e.g., 'A growth-theory-of-interest intervention increases interest in math and science coursework among liberal arts undergraduates,' adapted for younger students). We will track participants annually through high school, collecting administrative data on course enrollment, grades, and standardized test scores. Annually, we will administer validated self-report measures, including the Theories of Intelligence Scale and domain-specific belief surveys, to assess mindset maintenance. Data will be analyzed using latent growth curve modeling and cross-lagged panel analyses to test for sustained group differences and mediation pathways over time.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first longitudinal evidence on the multi-year durability of a growth-theory-of-interest intervention, moving beyond short-term efficacy. It will identify whether the intervention provides a lasting protective or accelerative effect through the critical high school transition. The findings will directly inform the design of future interventions, highlighting the potential need for booster sessions or contextual supports to maintain initial gains.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined mindset change over time, no pre-2026 study has specifically tracked the long-term academic trajectory of students who received a targeted *interest*-theory intervention from middle into high school, a key period for STEM pipeline development. This directly tests the 'sleeper effect' hypothesis for educational mindset interventions.

proposals.title: Scaling Growth Mindset: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Teacher-Led Intervention to Promote Academic Resilience in Middle School
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Dweck and others demonstrates that brief, researcher-delivered growth mindset interventions can improve student outcomes, particularly for struggling students. However, scaling these benefits to entire school systems is hindered by reliance on external experts. This project addresses the critical need to translate proven psychological principles into sustainable, teacher-implemented classroom practices to broaden impact and institutionalize a culture of growth.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, teacher-led classroom intervention, adapted from established incremental theory training, will be feasible for implementation and will significantly improve students' academic mindsets, challenge-seeking behaviors, and mathematics grades compared to a business-as-usual control.
proposals.method: We will conduct a school-level randomized controlled trial in 20 middle schools. Teachers in intervention schools will receive a standardized, scripted curriculum (four 25-minute lessons) and training via a one-day workshop and ongoing coaching. The intervention content, adapted from Yeager et al. (2019), will teach the malleability of intelligence through neuroscience analogies, student testimonials, and reflective writing exercises, but will be delivered by the regular classroom teacher. Fidelity will be monitored via teacher logs and observer checklists. Primary outcomes include pre/post changes in student mindset (Theory of Intelligence Scale), self-reported challenge-seeking, and end-of-semester math grades.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first rigorous efficacy test of a fully teacher-delivered growth mindset intervention at scale. It will yield a validated, manualized protocol for teacher implementation and critical data on the necessary conditions (e.g., training, support) for effective delivery, directly informing educational policy and professional development.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has trained teachers in growth mindset concepts, this study uniquely tests the efficacy of teachers directly delivering the structured psychoeducational content of a proven intervention, moving beyond a 'train-the-trainer' model to a 'teacher-as-interventionist' model for sustainable dissemination.

proposals.title: The Hidden Curriculum of Ability: A Mixed-Methods Investigation of Classroom Practices that Systematically Reinforce Entity Theories
proposals.motivation: Extensive pre-2026 research by Dweck and others demonstrates the benefits of growth mindsets for student resilience and achievement. However, most interventions focus on changing individual student beliefs.
