Far less is known about the specific, systemic features of the classroom environment that may unintentionally cultivate and reinforce fixed mindsets (entity theories), potentially undermining intervention efforts. Identifying these 'levers in the system' is crucial for designing sustainable, context-level changes in education.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, observable instructional and assessment practices—particularly those related to ability grouping, error handling, and the framing of feedback—will be systematically associated with a stronger student endorsement of entity theories about intelligence in that classroom.
proposals.method: We will conduct a sequential mixed-methods study in 30-40 diverse middle school classrooms. First, we will use ethnographic observation and teacher interviews to catalog and categorize common practices hypothesized to signal fixed-ability beliefs. Second, we will develop and validate a quantitative Classroom Entity-Theory Reinforcement (CETR) observation rubric. Finally, we will administer the validated CETR rubric alongside pre-post student surveys measuring mindset (using established scales) to test the correlation between observed practices and shifts in student entity beliefs over a semester, controlling for prior mindset and achievement.
proposals.contribution: This study will move the field from a primary focus on changing the individual to a systemic analysis of the learning context. It will provide the first validated observational tool for identifying mindset-reinforcing practices and generate a concrete taxonomy of modifiable classroom factors that educators and policymakers can target to create growth-mindset cultures, complementing existing student-focused interventions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has documented the *effects* of a fixed-mindset environment, this study uniquely seeks to identify and measure the *specific, observable teacher behaviors and structural practices* that constitute that environment, addressing a critical inverse problem in mindset theory.

proposals.title: Beyond the Classroom: Developing and Validating a Domain-Specific Mindset Scale for Adolescent Sports, Arts, and Social Skills
proposals.motivation: While mindset research has robustly explored academic and health domains, adolescence is a critical period where identity and competence are also forged in non-academic arenas like sports, arts, and peer relationships. Pre-2026 work by Dweck and others has shown that domain-specific mindsets are more predictive than general ones, yet no validated instrument exists to measure growth versus fixed mindsets across these key developmental contexts. This gap limits our ability to understand and support adolescent development holistically.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adolescents hold distinct, measurable mindsets (growth vs. fixed) specific to sports ability, artistic skill, and social competence, and that these domain-specific mindsets will predict resilience, effort, and improvement in their respective domains better than a general mindset measure.
proposals.method: Following the scale development and validation methodology used for the Illness Mindset Inventory (IMI), we will conduct a multi-phase, multi-cohort study. Phase 1 involves qualitative interviews with adolescents to generate item pools for each domain. Phase 2 employs exploratory factor analysis (EFA) on data from 500+ adolescents to refine scales. Phase 3 uses confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) on a separate sample to validate the factor structure and establish convergent/discriminant validity against established measures (e.g., Theories of Intelligence Scale, IMI). Phase 4 is a longitudinal study tracking domain-specific engagement and skill development over six months to test predictive validity.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first psychometrically validated tool for assessing mindsets in core non-academic adolescent domains. It will extend mindset theory by empirically mapping its applicability and predictive power across developmentally salient contexts, offering a more nuanced framework for understanding adolescent motivation and resilience. The resulting scale will enable targeted interventions to foster adaptive mindsets in sports, arts, and social settings.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond applying a general mindset construct to new domains by rigorously developing and validating *domain-specific* instruments for adolescent sports, arts, and social skills, an integration not yet attempted in pre-2026 literature. It tests whether the mindset framework operates with the same specificity and predictive power in these non-academic, performance-oriented contexts as it does in academics and health.

proposals.title: Testing the Sufficiency of Mindset: A Randomized Controlled Trial of Growth and Belonging Interventions in Under-Resourced Classrooms
proposals.motivation: Mindset interventions have shown promise in improving academic outcomes, but their success often occurs in contexts with adequate teaching and resources. This raises a critical, pre-2026 question: Can changing students' beliefs alone drive achievement when structural and instructional quality is demonstrably low? Understanding the limits of belief-focused interventions is essential for ethical application and for directing resources effectively in educational equity efforts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a growth mindset or social-belonging intervention, delivered in isolation without concurrent improvements to teaching quality or material resources, will be insufficient to produce significant gains in academic achievement for students in under-resourced classrooms.
proposals.method: We will conduct a school-randomized controlled trial in 30 middle schools with documented low teaching quality (via classroom observation scores) and resource constraints. Schools will be randomly assigned to: 1) a combined growth mindset and belonging intervention (two online sessions), 2) an active control (study skills). Pre- and post-measures will include standardized math achievement tests, the validated Implicit Theories of Intelligence Scale, and the School Belonging Scale. Primary analysis will use hierarchical linear modeling to test for condition-by-time interactions on achievement, controlling for baseline scores.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct experimental test of the boundary conditions of standalone mindset interventions. It will clarify whether belief change can be a sufficient lever for achievement or if it primarily functions as a catalyst within supportive environments, thereby informing more nuanced theories of educational intervention and practical implementation guidelines.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines mindset as a moderator of environmental effects, this study directly manipulates mindset in a context where environmental supports are intentionally held constant and low, testing its sufficiency rather than its interaction—a crucial but unexplored distinction.

proposals.title: The Long-Term Trajectory of a Growth Mindset: A 15-Year Longitudinal Study on College Persistence, Career Paths, and Lifelong Learning
proposals.motivation: While Dweck's research has robustly demonstrated that growth mindset interventions improve academic grades and motivation in the short term, their impact on major life-course decisions remains largely unmeasured. Understanding whether a growth mindset influences long-term educational persistence, career choice in challenging fields, and sustained engagement in learning is critical for evaluating the true societal value of these psychological interventions. This addresses a key gap in the pre-2026 literature, which lacks longitudinal data spanning from adolescence into early adulthood.
proposals.hypothesis: Individuals who endorse or are taught a growth mindset in adolescence will demonstrate greater college persistence (lower dropout rates), a higher propensity to enter and remain in STEM and other demanding careers, and more frequent engagement in formal and informal lifelong learning activities over a 15-year period compared to those with a fixed mindset.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal panel study, re-contacting participants from prior mindset intervention studies (e.g., the 2019 growth-theory-of-interest intervention cohort) and administering the validated Implicit Theories of Intelligence Scale and Illness Mindset Inventory at baseline. We will link self-report survey data on educational milestones, career choices, and learning activities with institutional records (with consent) for college enrollment and completion. Data analysis will employ survival analysis (Cox proportional hazards models) for persistence outcomes, multinomial logistic regression for career choice, and latent growth curve modeling to track engagement in learning over time, controlling for socioeconomic status and prior achievement.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal evidence directly linking growth mindset to concrete life outcomes beyond GPA, shifting the field's focus from academic performance to life trajectory. It will quantify the potential return on investment for educational mindset interventions and identify critical transition points (e.g., college major declaration, first career change) where mindset effects are most potent.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond the well-established domain of academic grades to test the foundational claim that mindsets shape life paths, using a rigorous longitudinal design that tracks actual behavioral outcomes over decades, not just self-reported beliefs or short-term academic performance.

proposals.title: Gamifying the Process: A Randomized Trial of a Growth-Mindset-Embedded Learning Platform on Student Persistence and Achievement
proposals.motivation: While growth mindset interventions have shown promise in education, their effects can be fleeting. Gamification is widely used in educational technology but often focuses on performance outcomes (e.g., points for correct answers), which can inadvertently reinforce a fixed mindset by rewarding innate ability. There is a critical need to design digital learning environments where the core mechanics systematically reinforce the belief that effort and strategy develop ability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a learning platform with gamification elements explicitly designed to reward effort, strategy use, and improvement (process-focused gamification) will lead to stronger growth mindset beliefs, greater challenge-seeking, and higher academic resilience compared to a platform with traditional outcome-focused gamification or no gamification.
proposals.method: We will develop and test a web-based mathematics learning module for middle school students. The experimental condition will feature: (1) an "Effort Energy" bar that fills based on time-on-task and attempts at challenging problems, unlocking hints; (2) "Strategy Badges" awarded for using multiple solution paths; and (3) a "Mastery Level" that increases only after demonstrating improvement from initial performance on a topic. A yoked control condition will receive points and badges for correct answers only. We will employ a three-arm randomized controlled trial (Process Gamification vs. Outcome Gamification vs. Non-Gamified Control) with pre/post measures of mindset (Theory of Intelligence Scale), behavioral persistence on an optional challenging task, and module performance.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a causal test of how specific game design mechanics influence motivational constructs. It will translate Dweck's theoretical framework into a concrete design architecture for educational technology, moving beyond broad labels of "gamification" to specify which features support adaptive learning beliefs. The findings could offer a scalable model for embedding growth mindset principles into digital learning tools.
proposals.novelty: Prior work has applied mindset interventions *alongside* learning or used general gamification. This study uniquely engineers the game mechanics themselves to be direct carriers of the growth mindset message, making the theory intrinsic to the platform's reward structure rather than an external add-on.

proposals.title: Precision Mindset Intervention: A Data-Driven Approach to Personalize Growth Mindset Support in Secondary Education
proposals.motivation: While growth mindset interventions show promise, their effects are heterogeneous, with some students benefiting more than others. This variability suggests a one-size-fits-all approach is suboptimal. Advances in educational data mining and the conceptual framework of precision medicine, which tailors medical treatment to individual patient characteristics, provide a novel lens to address this challenge in education.
proposals.hypothesis: A precision intervention model that uses baseline student data (e.g., prior academic performance, initial mindset scores, behavioral engagement metrics) to algorithmically personalize the timing and content of growth mindset supports will yield significantly greater improvements in students' mindsets, academic resilience, and course grades compared to a standardized, one-time intervention.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized controlled trial in secondary schools. The control group receives a standard online growth mindset module. The precision group's intervention is guided by a machine learning model (e.g., a random forest classifier) trained on pre-intervention data to predict 'intervention need' and 'optimal content type.' The system will use a multi-armed bandit algorithm to dynamically schedule follow-up micro-interventions (e.g., tailored stories, reflection prompts) via a learning platform based on real-time engagement and performance data, creating a personalized support sequence.
proposals.contribution: This research will develop and test a novel, scalable framework for delivering personalized psychological interventions in educational settings. It will move the field beyond static interventions toward dynamic, responsive support systems. The findings will provide concrete evidence for how data analytics can be ethically used to enhance the efficacy and efficiency of social-psychological interventions.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely integrates the precision medicine paradigm with mindset theory, using adaptive algorithms to personalize not just the content but the *timing* of interventions based on continuous data streams, a significant advance over prior fixed-schedule or broad-brush approaches.

proposals.title: Embedding Growth: A Randomized Trial of Teacher-Delivered Micro-Interventions to Foster Growth Mindsets in Daily Mathematics Instruction
proposals.motivation: While mindset interventions have shown efficacy, they often exist as separate, scripted modules, limiting scalability and integration into the regular curriculum. Pre-2026 research highlights the challenge of sustaining intervention effects and the need for teachers to become natural carriers of growth mindset messaging. This study addresses the critical gap of translating core mindset principles into sustainable, teacher-led practices within the authentic flow of classroom instruction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that teachers trained to deliver brief, context-specific growth mindset cues (e.g., in feedback, during struggle) will foster stronger growth mindsets and greater challenge-seeking behavior in their students compared to teachers in an active control condition delivering general encouragement.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cluster-randomized controlled trial in 40 middle school mathematics classrooms. Teachers in the intervention arm will receive a structured professional development workshop and a digital 'cue library' of micro-strategies (e.g., framing errors as learning opportunities, using 'not yet' language, praising process). Fidelity will be monitored via brief weekly teacher surveys and audio recordings of lessons coded for cue frequency and quality. Student outcomes (implicit theories of intelligence, behavioral challenge-seeking on optional problems, and grades) will be measured pre- and post-intervention over one academic semester.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a validated, practical protocol for embedding growth mindset cues into routine teaching, moving beyond add-on interventions. It will provide evidence for the causal impact of teacher-delivered micro-messaging on student mindsets and behavior, offering a more sustainable model for educational practice.
proposals.novelty: This proposal shifts the unit of intervention from a student-focused session to the teacher's moment-to-minute instructional language, testing the potency of highly contextualized, distributed cues rather than a consolidated lesson.

proposals.title: Fostering Growth and Belonging Mindsets in Workplace Training: A Randomized Controlled Trial to Enhance Skill Acquisition and Team Integration
proposals.motivation: Organizations invest heavily in training, yet outcomes are often suboptimal due to trainees' fixed mindsets about their abilities and feelings of not belonging, especially among underrepresented groups. Pre-2026 research by Dweck and others shows mindsets powerfully influence learning and resilience in academic and health domains, but systematic application in corporate training is underexplored. Extending this framework to adult professional development addresses a critical gap with significant implications for workforce adaptability and equity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a brief, scalable online intervention teaching a growth mindset (abilities can be developed) and a belonging mindset (setbacks in training are normal and temporary) will lead to greater persistence, better skill acquisition, and stronger social integration during a workplace training program compared to a control intervention.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial with new hires undergoing a standardized technical training program at a partner corporation. Participants (target N=400) will be randomly assigned to receive either: 1) our growth-and-belonging mindset intervention, featuring written narratives and reflection exercises from senior employees, or 2) a control module on general brain functions. Primary outcomes will be objective post-training skill assessment scores and peer-rated team integration. Secondary outcomes include self-reported persistence on challenging tasks and training completion rates. Analyses will use ANCOVA, controlling for baseline skills, to test for between-group differences.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causal evidence for a combined growth-and-belonging mindset intervention in a real-world corporate training context. It will yield a validated, brief intervention protocol that organizations can deploy to improve training efficacy and inclusivity. The findings will extend mindset theory beyond educational and health psychology into organizational behavior and adult learning.
proposals.novelty: While mindset interventions exist for students and patients, applying a dual-mindset (growth + belonging) framework to adult workplace training is novel. It moves beyond academic performance to target the concrete, high-stakes domains of professional skill acquisition and team dynamics, testing the generalizability of core theory.

proposals.title: How Early Interpretations of Success Shape Trajectories: A 10-Year Longitudinal Study of Mindset and Attributional Patterns
proposals.motivation: While Dweck's pre-2026 work established that mindsets influence resilience and motivation, the long-term developmental pathways are less clear. It is critical to understand whether early, subtle differences in how children cognitively process success—as evidence of innate talent versus effortful growth—cascade to predict significant divergence in academic persistence, career choices, and well-being over a decade.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that children who, at age 10-12, interpret academic successes as indicative of malleable ability (a growth-oriented attribution) will demonstrate higher academic attainment, greater vocational adaptability, and better psychological well-being at age 20-22, compared to peers who interpret similar successes as evidence of fixed traits.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 10-year longitudinal study with a cohort of 500 middle-school students. At baseline (T1), we will use validated scenario-based measures and the Implicit Theories Scale to assess success attributions. Annual surveys will track academic behaviors, challenge-seeking, and mindset. At decade follow-up (T2), we will measure objective outcomes (college completion, GPA, career data) and psychological well-being. Analyses will use latent growth curve modeling and structural equation modeling to test how T1 attribution patterns predict T2 outcomes, controlling for baseline achievement and socioeconomic status.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal evidence directly linking specific cognitive patterns of success interpretation to major life outcomes a decade later. It will extend mindset theory beyond laboratory and short-term educational interventions, demonstrating its foundational role in life-span development. The findings could inform the timing and targeting of mindset interventions for maximal long-term impact.
proposals.novelty: Prior longitudinal work often examines mindset as a static predictor; this study uniquely focuses on the dynamic *attributional process* following success as the key mechanistic predictor, tracking its effects across a critical developmental period from adolescence into early adulthood.

proposals.title: Scaling Mindsets: A Growth Mindset Intervention Reduces Threat Appraisals and Improves Resilience to Social Rejection in Middle School Children
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Dweck and others has robustly shown that a growth mindset of intelligence improves academic resilience. However, the domain-specificity of mindsets suggests that these benefits may not automatically transfer to the critical social domain. Given the profound impact of social stress and peer rejection on adolescent mental health and academic engagement, it is crucial to test whether the core principles of the success/failure paradigm can be effectively scaled to children's interpretations of social challenges.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that teaching children a growth mindset of social attributes—that social skills and peer relationships can be developed—will lead them to interpret social setbacks (e.g., rejection, conflict) as opportunities for learning rather than as permanent, self-defining failures, thereby increasing their behavioral resilience and prosocial coping strategies.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized controlled trial with ~400 middle school students. The intervention group will complete a series of three online, interactive modules adapted from the growth mindset of intelligence intervention, but reframed for the social domain (e.g., stories about the brain forming new connections when navigating social challenges, testimonials on overcoming rejection). The control group will receive modules on general brain function. Pre-, post-, and 6-week follow-up assessments will measure: 1) social mindset (using a newly validated scale), 2) threat appraisals and affect following a simulated social rejection vignette (Cyberball paradigm), and 3) teacher-reported prosocial behavior and peer conflict resolution.
proposals.contribution: This study will extend mindset theory by providing the first rigorous test of a scalable, domain-specific social mindset intervention. It will contribute a validated measure of social mindsets and causal evidence for whether changing these beliefs can buffer against the negative psychological and behavioral impacts of peer rejection, a key risk factor in adolescence.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined mindsets in academic and health domains (e.g., illness mindsets), systematically applying the success/failure learning paradigm to the nuanced, dynamic context of peer interactions represents a novel and non-obvious domain extension, moving beyond trait-focused social psychology to a malleable, process-oriented framework.

proposals.title: Testing the Causal Primacy of Mindsets: An Experimental Investigation of Beliefs as Justifications for Performance Depletion
proposals.motivation: Mindset theory posits that core beliefs (e.g., fixed vs. growth) causally influence resilience and performance under challenge. However, an alternative, under-explored explanation from social psychology is that self-reported beliefs may sometimes serve as post-hoc justifications for one's performance, rather than being the primary driver. Disentangling this is crucial for refining theory and ensuring interventions target true causal mechanisms, not epiphenomena.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that experimentally induced performance outcomes (success vs. failure on a demanding task) will significantly shift participants' subsequent self-reported mindset endorsements, particularly for domain-specific mindsets, supporting the view that beliefs can be constructed as justifications.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 2x2 between-subjects experiment manipulating both pre-task mindset priming (growth vs. fixed) and task performance feedback (success vs. failure). Participants will complete a rigorous, multi-stage cognitive depletion task (e.g., sequential tough puzzles). The key dependent variable is the post-feedback Illness Mindset Inventory (IMI) adapted for 'cognitive fatigue.' We will use mediation and moderation analyses (PROCESS Model 7) to test if feedback effect on IMI scores is mediated by self-efficacy change and moderated by the initial priming condition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a critical test of the causal directionality between mindsets and outcomes in a depletion context. It will advance mindset theory by either robustly confirming its causal assumptions or identifying boundary conditions where beliefs are more malleable and reactive than previously thought, guiding more precise intervention design.
proposals.novelty: While prior work shows mindsets predict outcomes, this study directly tests if the reverse causal path—outcomes shaping mindset reports—is potent, challenging the assumed primacy of beliefs. It innovatively applies the validated IMI framework to a novel domain (cognitive depletion) to test this theoretical alternative.

proposals.title: Cultural Foundations of Willpower Beliefs: Testing the Influence of Individualism-Collectivism on Lay Theories of Ego Depletion
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on lay theories of willpower, pioneered by Dweck and colleagues, shows that believing willpower is limited (vs. non-limited) predicts worse self-regulation and greater ego depletion effects. However, this work has been conducted primarily in Western, individualistic contexts. Given that core psychological processes like self-regulation are shaped by cultural values, it is critical to test whether these foundational beliefs vary systematically across cultures, particularly along the individualism-collectivism dimension. This investigation matters for developing culturally inclusive models of motivation and for designing effective mindset interventions globally.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individuals from more collectivistic cultural backgrounds will be more likely to endorse a non-limited theory of willpower, viewing self-control as fueled by social and duty-based motivations, compared to individuals from more individualistic backgrounds, who will be more likely to endorse a limited theory, viewing willpower as a finite personal resource.
proposals.method: We will conduct a large-scale, cross-sectional survey study across at least six countries pre-selected for high variation on Hofstede's individualism index (e.g., U.S., Germany, Japan, China, South Korea, Mexico). We will recruit 300 participants per country via validated online panels. Participants will complete the Implicit Theories of Willpower Scale (Job et al., 2010) and a validated measure of individualism-collectivism (e.g., Singelis's Self-Construal Scale). We will use multi-group confirmatory factor analysis to establish measurement invariance of the willpower scale across cultures. Primary analysis will employ hierarchical linear modeling (HLM) with country-level individualism scores as a Level-2 predictor of individual-level willpower beliefs (Level-1), controlling for demographics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic evidence on how cultural values shape fundamental beliefs about self-regulation. It will extend Dweck's mindset theory into the cross-cultural domain, specifically for willpower beliefs. The findings will inform the potential boundary conditions of the ego depletion effect and are essential for tailoring growth mindset interventions about willpower to be culturally resonant.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined willpower beliefs as individual differences, no research has explicitly tested their variation as a function of broader, ecologically valid cultural dimensions. This moves beyond comparing mean scores between nations to model how a specific cultural syndrome (individualism-collectivism) predicts the prevalence of a specific lay theory.

proposals.title: Sustaining the Limited to Unlimited: A Multi-Component Intervention for Long-Term Change in Willpower Mindsets
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research shows that believing willpower is limited (a 'limited-resource theory') predicts poorer self-regulation and goal pursuit under challenge compared to believing it is unlimited. While brief interventions can shift these lay theories, their effects often decay, limiting real-world impact. Developing scalable, durable interventions to instill a 'non-limited' or 'growth' theory of willpower is crucial for promoting sustained resilience in health, education, and personal domains.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-component intervention that combines a direct mindset-training module with structured behavioral practice and environmental cueing will produce a more durable shift toward a non-limited theory of willpower and lead to greater long-term improvements in self-regulation and goal attainment than a single-session educational intervention.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal randomized controlled trial with three conditions: 1) Active Control (AC), 2) Single-Session Mindset (SSM), and 3) Multi-Component Intervention (MCI). The MCI will include: a) an adapted SSM module teaching the science of willpower as malleable; b) a 4-week 'willpower workout' program where participants practice self-regulation in progressively challenging, personalized tasks while reflecting on capacity-building; and c) digital environmental supports (e.g., SMS reminders of the non-limited mindset). We will recruit adults striving for a health or academic goal (N=450), assessing willpower mindset (adapted IMI), behavioral outcomes, and well-being at pre-test, post-test, 3-month, and 12-month follow-ups.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a tested, manualized protocol for creating lasting belief change about a core self-regulatory capacity. It will advance the field of mindset interventions beyond one-shot paradigms by demonstrating the efficacy of a theory-based, sustained support model. Findings will offer practical insights for integrating mindset science into long-term coaching, therapy, and educational programs.
proposals.novelty: While prior work tests initial mindset change, this proposal uniquely focuses on *sustaining* that change by integrating explicit mindset training with deliberate practice and environmental supports, a synthesis not yet empirically tested in the willpower domain. It also extends the Illness Mindset Inventory framework to track the dynamics of belief change over a full year.

proposals.title: Domain-Specificity in Willpower Theories: Development and Validation of the Domain-Specific Willpower Beliefs Scale
proposals.motivation: Research on implicit theories of willpower has established its impact on self-regulation and goal pursuit, but it has largely treated willpower as a global belief. This is a critical gap, as pre-2026 work by Dweck and others on mindsets in illness, academics, and intelligence demonstrates that beliefs are often domain-specific. Understanding if and how willpower beliefs vary across domains like diet, work, and emotion regulation is essential for designing precise, effective interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: Individuals hold distinct, measurable implicit theories about willpower (limited vs. non-limited) that are specific to life domains (e.g., dietary control, cognitive work, emotional regulation), and these domain-specific beliefs will predict self-regulatory outcomes in their respective domains better than a global willpower belief measure.
proposals.method: Following the methodological framework used in developing the Illness Mindset Inventory, we will conduct a multi-study scale development and validation process. First, qualitative interviews will generate items for distinct domains (diet, work, emotion). Then, using exploratory and confirmatory factor analysis on separate samples, we will test the factor structure of the Domain-Specific Willpower Beliefs Scale (DSWBS). Finally, longitudinal and experimental studies will establish predictive and incremental validity, correlating domain-specific scores with corresponding behavioral outcomes (e.g., dietary logs, academic persistence, emotion regulation tasks) and testing their malleability through targeted interventions.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first validated instrument to measure domain-specific willpower theories, extending mindset theory into a new dimension of specificity. It will offer a more nuanced predictive model for self-regulation failures and successes, directly informing the design of targeted interventions (e.g., for weight management, academic support, or mental health) that address the specific belief hindering progress in a given area.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has shown willpower theories are malleable and consequential, it has not systematically investigated whether these beliefs are monolithic or compartmentalized by domain, a non-obvious possibility given the domain-specificity found in other mindset literatures.

proposals.title: Theories of Others' Willpower: How Lay Beliefs About Malleability Influence Demands and Support in Dyadic Interactions
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on mindsets has robustly shown that an individual's own beliefs (e.g., about intelligence or willpower) shape their self-regulation and resilience. However, social interactions are often governed by our perceptions of others' capacities. Understanding how people's implicit theories about *another person's* willpower—whether it is seen as a limited or expandable resource—affects interpersonal demands and support is critical for domains like management, healthcare, education, and caregiving, where such judgments directly impact outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individuals who hold a 'limited' theory of others' willpower will impose fewer subsequent demands on a partner after a taxing task and offer more unsolicited support, compared to those who hold an 'expandable' theory, who will maintain or increase demands, viewing effort as strengthening willpower.
proposals.method: We will adapt the validated methods from Dweck's lab on measuring and manipulating implicit theories. First, we will develop and validate a 'Theories of Others' Willpower' scale. Then, in a dyadic laboratory experiment, one participant (the 'perceiver') will be primed with either a limited or expandable theory of willpower via a scientific article paradigm. They will then observe their partner (the 'target') complete a strenuous self-control task (e.g., the 'e' cross-out task). Finally, the perceiver will be given the opportunity to assign a subsequent, optional task to the target and their supportive vs. directive communications will be coded. Analyses will use ANOVA and mediation models to test the primary hypothesis.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend mindset theory into the novel, critical domain of social perception and interpersonal behavior. It will provide the first empirical evidence that lay theories about others' traits directly shape social demands and support strategies, bridging the gap between personal mindset research and applied social psychology. The findings could inform interventions for improving supervisory, instructional, and caregiving practices.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines personal willpower mindsets and illness mindsets about others, this study uniquely investigates lay theories about a *fundamental psychological capacity* (willpower) in others and their behavioral consequences, a previously unexplored intersection.

proposals.title: Shifting the Social Ecology of Mindsets: A School-Level Randomized Trial to Alter Peer Norms About Intellectual Growth
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Dweck and others demonstrates that individual mindset interventions can improve academic resilience, but effect sizes are variable and scaling is challenging. A critical, under-explored barrier is that a student's growth mindset may be undermined by fixed-mindset peer norms within their school. This study addresses the ecological context of belief change, testing whether directly altering perceived school-wide norms is a more powerful and sustainable lever for change than focusing solely on individual student beliefs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a school-wide intervention designed to shift perceived peer norms toward the malleability of intelligence will produce greater and more equitable gains in students' challenge-seeking behaviors and academic persistence than a traditional intervention targeting individual student mindsets.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cluster-randomized controlled trial in 20 middle schools. Schools will be randomized to: 1) a Norms Condition, featuring school-wide assemblies, teacher-led discussions of school survey data showing most peers believe intelligence can grow, and visible public commitments; 2) an Individual Condition, delivering the standard online growth mindset module to students; or 3) a Control Condition. Primary outcomes are behavioral measures: choice of a challenging vs. easy task and GPA in core STEM subjects. We will use multilevel modeling (HLM) to analyze nested student data, testing for condition-by-time interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend mindset theory into the social-ecological domain, providing a novel framework for scalable educational interventions. It will offer concrete evidence on whether shifting peer norms is a more effective pathway to widespread behavioral change than individual belief modification, with implications for reducing inequality by creating supportive belief environments.
proposals.novelty: While prior work acknowledges social context, this study innovates by making the school-wide normative environment the direct and primary target of intervention, rather than a secondary consideration or moderator of individual-level treatment effects.

proposals.title: Beyond Grades: A Randomized Trial Testing the Impact of a Growth Mindset Intervention on Psychological Well-being and Social Belonging in College Students
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on growth mindset interventions has predominantly validated their efficacy through academic metrics like grades and persistence. However, Dweck's core theory posits that mindsets are foundational belief systems that should influence broader domains of functioning. It remains an open question whether these interventions might have their most significant or immediate impact on non-academic outcomes critical to student development, such as mental health and social integration, which are pressing concerns in higher education.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a brief, online growth mindset of intelligence intervention will lead to greater improvements in psychological well-being (reduced stress, anxiety) and perceived social belonging among first-year college students over a semester, compared to a control intervention, and that these effects may be stronger than its effects on academic performance in the same timeframe.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pre-registered, double-blind randomized controlled trial with ~500 first-year undergraduates. Participants will be randomly assigned to complete either a validated growth mindset of intelligence module or a control module on brain function. Using validated pre-2026 scales, we will measure primary outcomes of psychological well-being (e.g., DASS-21) and social belonging at baseline, immediate post-test, and 6-week follow-up. Academic outcomes (course grades, continued enrollment) will be collected as secondary endpoints. Analyses will use linear mixed models to test for Condition x Time interactions on the well-being and belonging measures, and will compare effect sizes across outcome domains.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, longitudinal test of whether a growth mindset intervention primarily designed to improve academic attitudes confers significant benefits for mental health and social integration. It will expand the applied scope of mindset theory beyond educational achievement into holistic student wellness. The findings could inform more integrative student support programs that target foundational beliefs to promote resilience across multiple life domains.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links mindsets to well-being correlates, no pre-2026 study has explicitly designed an RCT to test if a standard academic mindset intervention is a more potent lever for improving mental health and belonging than for improving grades, thereby challenging the field's primary endpoint assumption.

proposals.title: The Impact of a Fixed-Mindset Prime on Challenge-Seeking and Persistence: A Controlled Experimental Study
proposals.motivation: Dweck's pre-2026 research robustly demonstrates the benefits of growth mindset interventions. However, the field lacks a precise, ethically controlled understanding of the baseline state these interventions shift people *from*. Testing the inverse effect of a fixed-mindset message is crucial for calibrating the true magnitude and mechanisms of growth mindset effects, providing a more complete scientific model of how beliefs influence behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that participants exposed to a brief, ethically-framed fixed-mindset prime will subsequently demonstrate lower challenge-seeking (e.g., choosing easier tasks) and reduced persistence on a difficult, unrelated task compared to a neutral control group.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized, between-subjects online experiment (N=300). Participants will be randomly assigned to read a short, scientifically-toned article framing intellectual ability as either fixed (experimental) or neutral (control, about brain anatomy). The fixed-mindset prime will be carefully framed as 'one perspective' for ethical integrity. Dependent measures will include a validated behavioral choice task (selecting a challenging vs. easy problem set) and a timed persistence task on an impossible puzzle. Data will be analyzed using ANOVA and mediation analysis to test for direct effects and the role of post-prime mindset beliefs.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, quantitative estimate of the behavioral cost of a fixed mindset in a controlled setting. It will strengthen the theoretical foundation of mindset theory by explicitly modeling the 'push' from a fixed baseline and the 'pull' of a growth intervention. The findings will also inform the design of more potent growth interventions by clarifying the specific deficits they must address.
proposals.novelty: While prior work compares growth interventions to neutral controls, this proposal directly manipulates the fixed-mindset baseline, a novel and methodologically rigorous approach to deconstructing the total effect of mindset messaging. It applies the ethical and methodological framework of Dweck's recent illness and cancer mindset trials to the core academic domain.

proposals.title: Cultivating a Context-Insensitive Growth Mindset: A Dual-Problem Intervention to Buffer Students from Unsupportive Peer Norms
proposals.motivation: Prior research demonstrates that students' growth mindsets can be undermined by unsupportive peer norms, such as a fixed-mindset culture that stigmatizes effort. Changing entire social environments is often impractical. Therefore, a critical gap exists: can we directly strengthen a student's resilience to such negative contexts, making their growth mindset and its benefits more robust?
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a targeted intervention teaching students to view unsupportive peer norms as a separate, external 'problem to be navigated'—rather than a valid signal about their own abilities—will reduce the negative impact of these norms on their challenge-seeking, persistence, and academic performance.
proposals.method: We will develop and test a randomized controlled 'Dual-Problem Intervention' for undergraduates. The intervention adapts established growth mindset and stress-is-enhancing content, but adds a novel module. This module uses psychoeducation and structured reflection exercises to help students (1) identify a 'growth vs. fixed' peer norm as a contextual problem distinct from their personal learning problem, and (2) strategize ways to protect their growth mindset (e.g., selective attention, re-appraisal). Efficacy will be tested against active control in a longitudinal study measuring mindset beliefs, sensitivity to peer cues, and academic behaviors.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend mindset theory by introducing the concept of 'contextual resilience' as a learnable belief. It will provide a scalable, person-centered intervention model that complements broader culture-change efforts, offering a practical tool for students in suboptimal learning environments.
proposals.novelty: While existing interventions aim to change personal mindsets or change environments, this proposal is novel in targeting the *interaction*—specifically, inoculating the personal mindset against the environment. It shifts the focus from fixing the context to fortifying the individual's interpretation of it.

ideas.title: Cultural Moderators of Praise Effects: An Ecological Validity Framework from Agronomy
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that effort praise is universally optimal by drawing an analogy from agronomy, where the universal benefit of a treatment (fertilizer) was disproven by ecological moderators. The analogous solution involved multi-site field trials across diverse environments. Adapted to psychology, this means designing a large-scale, ecologically valid study across numerous cultures, not just comparing East-West dichotomies. Researchers would implement standardized protocols for delivering effort vs. intelligence praise in real-world educational settings (e.g., classrooms), while rigorously measuring pre-existing cultural value dimensions as moderating variables. The goal is to produce a predictive 'map' showing which cultural configurations make effort praise optimal, neutral, or even detrimental for motivation, thereby replacing a universal claim with a context-contingent model.

ideas.title: Cognitive Immunization through Effort Praise: A Behavioral Vaccine Model for At-Risk Students
ideas.description: This research seed adapts a solution from immunology and preventive psychology—the inoculation model—to the educational context. In immunology, vaccines trigger protective responses; analogously, in 'stress inoculation training,' controlled exposure to stress builds resilience. The adapted idea proposes testing a 'motivational inoculation' protocol for at-risk students. The intervention would administer precise, timed 'doses' of process-focused effort praise immediately after students experience small, designed failures in safe, low-stakes learning activities. The core hypothesis is that this controlled pairing of effort (not success) with positive reinforcement following minor setbacks will function as a cognitive-behavioral 'vaccine,' strengthening students' associative pathways to buffer their motivation and self-efficacy against the demotivating impact of future, larger academic challenges or failures.

ideas.title: Cognitive Immunization: Adapting Vaccine Trial Designs to Test Temperament as a Buffer Against Negative Person Feedback
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core methodology from epidemiology and vaccine development to investigate how child characteristics moderate vulnerability to person feedback. In vaccine trials, a pre-administered vaccine's efficacy in preventing disease after pathogen exposure is the model. Here, a child's pre-existing temperamental trait (like high effortful control) or prior belief (like a growth mindset) is conceptualized as a 'cognitive vaccine.' The adapted research would use a longitudinal or experimental design where this protective factor is measured first. Children would then be exposed to a controlled, standardized instance of negative person feedback. By comparing the outcomes (e.g., self-worth, persistence, affect) between children high and low in the protective trait, we can rigorously test the causal moderating role of the characteristic, quantifying its 'buffering efficacy' against the harmful social 'pathogen.'

It then implements a longitudinal, multi-condition field experiment comparing the co-designed effort praise against strategy-focused praise and neutral, autonomy-supportive acknowledgment. Crucially, the design includes embedded measures of perceived authenticity, trust in the instructor, and private changes in self-concept to detect and understand any iatrogenic effects, ensuring the intervention genuinely supports motivation without unintended backlash.

ideas.title: Developmental Trajectory of Feedback Harm in High-Stakes Competition
ideas.description: This research investigates how the comparative impact of person-focused and outcome-focused feedback shifts from adolescence through adulthood in sustained, high-stakes competitive environments (e.g., academic tracking, professional sports, career advancement). It tests the hypothesis that while person feedback may be more damaging for adolescents due to identity formation, outcome feedback becomes disproportionately more harmful for adults, as repeated failure feedback in high-stakes settings corrodes the stable self-concept and perceived competence they have built. The study employs longitudinal and cross-sectional designs to disentangle age and experience effects, ultimately aiming to generate lifespan-specific guidelines for constructive feedback in competitive domains.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Theory to Test the Cultural Robustness of Person Praise Effects
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that person praise (praising innate ability) is universally harmful by using an analogy from ecology. In ecology, to determine if a trait is universally maladaptive, scientists conduct transplant experiments across distinct environmental niches. Adapting this, the research would not simply compare collectivist and individualist cultures as broad categories. Instead, it would define specific cultural 'niches' using precise, measurable dimensions (e.g., interdependence, relational mobility, social rigidity). Within these niches, a standardized experiment would 'transplant' the person-praise intervention and measure its psychological 'fitness' outcomes (e.g., post-failure persistence, learning goals, stress). This method allows for a rigorous test of whether the negative effect is robust across all human cultural environments or if its impact varies systematically with specific cultural parameters, offering a more nuanced, ecological understanding of praise.

ideas.title: Adapting Cognitive Load Measurement from Educational Psychology to Assess Implicit Self-Worth
ideas.description: This idea expands the original IAT-based method by drawing an analogy from educational psychology's approach to measuring implicit cognitive load. In that field, secondary-task reaction times are used as an objective, real-time measure of mental effort that bypasses the limitations of explicit self-report. Adapted to the context of self-worth, the research would involve participants receiving positive or negative feedback on a task. Following this, while they complete a primary task related to the feedback domain (e.g., another similar puzzle), they must also respond to intermittent, neutral sensory probes. The latency of these probe responses acts as the dependent variable. Increased reaction times (indicating cognitive interference) after negative versus positive feedback would provide a continuous, implicit behavioral measure of contingent self-worth activation, triangulating with IAT scores and explicit self-reports to offer a more comprehensive assessment.

ideas.title: Assessing Feedback Effects on Crystallized Cognitive Schemas
ideas.description: This idea adapts a methodological solution from developmental cognitive neuroscience, where researchers assess how interventions impact crystallized versus plastic cognitive schemas by comparing different age cohorts with longitudinal neuroimaging and behavioral tasks. The adapted research idea is to treat self-concept as a crystallizing schema and test if person feedback effects persist or intensify in adolescent and adult populations. A longitudinal design would compare these cohorts using pre- and post-feedback measures of self-concept stability (via self-discrepancy tasks and neural correlates of self-referential processing) to determine whether feedback induces plasticity in these more established schemas or is resisted, and whether any effects are amplified by the greater cognitive resources and more complex self-narratives present in older individuals.

ideas.title: Cognitive Immunization: Training Internal Dialogue to Build Resilience to Setbacks
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from immunology, where vaccines use controlled exposure to build immunity, this research adapts the solution to the psychological context of children's internal dialogue. The analogous solution from vaccinology involves introducing a weakened stimulus to train a defensive system. The adapted idea is a 'cognitive immunization' protocol: children undergo structured training where they practice specific scripts of self-administered person-focused and process-focused talk in response to mild, simulated failures or frustrations. The study would test if this deliberate, low-stakes practice of dual internal dialogue functions like a psychological vaccine, 'inoculating' children by building resilient cognitive habits that enable more adaptive coping when they later encounter authentic setbacks, thereby scaling the beneficial effects of self-directed praise and criticism.

ideas.title: The Managerial Feedback Paradox: When Process-Focused Feedback Undermines Motivation Through Relational and Attributional Pathways
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the person/process/outcome feedback framework with an investigation into the boundary conditions of process feedback's benefits. It proposes a study examining how adult employees' motivation is affected not just by the focus of managerial feedback (person, process, outcome), but by the interaction of that focus with the delivery quality (specific/constructive vs. vague/punitive) and the employee's perception of the manager's intent and competence. The novel synergy tests the hypothesis that poorly delivered process criticism can be more demotivating than outcome criticism, as it may be attributed to a fixed mindset or relational disregard from the manager, thereby damaging self-efficacy and trust.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Neuro-Behavioral Tracking of Contingent Self-Worth Formation in Ecologically Valid Performance Contexts
ideas.description: This study investigates how neural correlates of self-worth contingency emerge and stabilize through repeated, meaningful feedback in real-world goal pursuits. Participants engage in a multi-week personalized challenge (e.g., learning a skill) with periodic fMRI sessions where they receive process- or person-focused feedback from a real mentor, interleaved with daily ecological momentary assessments tracking affect and self-appraisal. The design analyzes how neural sensitivity to feedback type (person vs. process) in circuits like the ventral striatum and medial prefrontal cortex changes over time and predicts downstream behavioral resilience or disengagement. This approach moves beyond snapshots of brain activity to model the dynamic construction of contingent self-worth.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Study of Managerial Feedback Frameworks on Employee Trajectories and Organizational Culture
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the longitudinal, developmental methodology of tracking praise types in children with the organizational behavior framework of person/process/outcome feedback. It would track employees and their managers over multiple years to examine how consistent use of a specific feedback framework (e.g., person-focused praise akin to intelligence praise, versus process-focused praise akin to effort praise) shapes not only individual employee motivation, performance, and career resilience, but also propagates through teams to influence collective norms, communication patterns, and the broader organizational culture. The study uniquely examines feedback as a cultural seed that creates divergent long-term pathways for both individual professional development and systemic organizational behavior.

ideas.title: Inverse Inference of Parental Mindset via Bayesian Belief Updating from Feedback Traces
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to inverse problems from geophysics to the psychological domain. The core challenge is analogous: inferring hidden internal properties (parental beliefs about intelligence) from indirect, observable traces (their patterns of praise and criticism toward their child). The adapted method involves constructing a forward model that simulates how a parent with a specific belief state (e.g., a probabilistic weighting of growth vs. fixed mindset, and thresholds for interpreting child performance) would generate feedback across various child outcomes. Given a longitudinal dataset of real parent-child interactions, a Bayesian inversion framework is then employed. This framework treats parental mindset as a set of latent parameters to be estimated, iteratively comparing the model's output to the observed feedback data to infer the posterior distribution of those belief parameters, thereby solving the psychological 'inverse problem' and quantifying inference uncertainty.

ideas.title: The Contingent Value of Process Feedback: How Individual and Contextual Factors Moderate Its Impact
ideas.description: This research investigates the conditional effectiveness of process feedback by testing how its impact is moderated by recipient and situational variables. It experimentally manipulates the quality of process feedback delivery while measuring pre-existing traits (e.g., feedback orientation, core self-evaluations) and perceived context (e.g., trust in source, learning climate). The study analyzes the interaction effects between feedback type and these moderators on cognitive, affective, and behavioral outcomes. The goal is to produce a contingent model that predicts when and for whom even poorly delivered process feedback can be beneficial versus harmful.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Resilience Theory to Feedback Moderation in Competitive Systems
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that outcome-focused feedback is the primary driver of improvement in high-stakes competition. Drawing an analogy from Ecology and Resilience Theory, where managing for system resilience (rather than a single output) prevents catastrophic collapse, the solution is adapted. In the original ecological context, managers avoid optimizing for a single metric and instead introduce intermediate disturbances and focus on functional diversity to build adaptive capacity. Adapted to the research context, this translates to testing 'structured process feedback' against pure outcome feedback. This feedback modality would present the competitive outcome (win/loss) but immediately contextualize it within an analysis of the adaptable strategies, decision points, and controllable variables the performer used. The core hypothesis is that this method, by preventing the outcome from being perceived as a definitive judgment of fixed ability, will mitigate the documented harms of person-focused feedback while avoiding the demotivating and anxiety-inducing effects of stark outcome feedback in high-stakes environments, thereby fostering greater resilience and long-term performance.

ideas.title: Adapting Immunological Memory Models to Neurocognitive Praise Feedback
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from immunology. In immunology, the distinction between innate and adaptive immune memory—how the system learns from exposure to produce tolerance or a stronger response—provides a parallel for how the brain might learn from social feedback like praise. The analogous solution involves tracking specific biomarkers over time following a challenge. Adapted to neurocognitive research, the study would use longitudinal neuroimaging (fMRI/EEG) to model intelligence and effort praise as distinct cognitive stimuli that prime different neural systems. By exposing participants to praise and then, in a later session, to a failure task, researchers can identify and compare the neural 'memory traces' and reactivity patterns (e.g., in reward and error-processing networks) to see if effort praise fosters a more adaptive, resilient neurocognitive response to failure, akin to a protective adaptive immune memory, while intelligence praise leaves a more rigid and vulnerable one.

ideas.title: Manipulated and Measured: An Experimental Paradigm for Audible Self-Talk and Persistence
ideas.description: This study uses a controlled experimental design to directly manipulate the valence and focus (person vs. process) of children's self-administered talk following failure. Children are first trained in one of four self-talk styles (person-praise, person-criticism, process-praise, process-criticism) and then complete a challenging computer task designed to induce periodic setbacks. During the task, they are prompted to verbalize their assigned self-talk aloud, creating an objective behavioral measure. The primary outcomes are subsequent persistence on the task and physiological markers of stress recovery, providing a robust test of how experimentally induced self-talk styles affect coping behaviors beyond subjective report.

ideas.title: Real-Time Behavioral Economics of Praise: Using EMA to Trigger and Measure Incentive Shifts
ideas.description: This research integrates Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) with behavioral economic games to study the real-time causal impact of praise. Participants report natural praise encounters through an EMA app, which then immediately administers a brief, incentivized game (e.g., a risk-taking or investment task). This design captures the immediate affective and cognitive response to praise (Idea A) and uses it as the independent variable to quantify its effect on subsequent goal-directed behavior and decision-making (Idea B). The synergy enables a micro-longitudinal, within-person analysis of how the quality, source, and context of real-world praise dynamically alter economic preferences and performance incentives as they unfold in daily life.

ideas.title: The Feedback Contingency Matrix: Mapping the Interaction of Feedback Focus and Delivery Quality on Motivation in High-Stakes Competition
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the two ideas to investigate the joint impact of feedback focus and delivery quality in high-stakes competitive environments. It proposes a 2x2 experimental design crossing feedback focus (outcome vs. process) with delivery quality (specific/constructive vs. vague/poorly delivered) to test their interactive effects on motivation, self-efficacy, and performance. The core hypothesis is that the presumed benefits of process feedback are contingent on its delivery, and the harms of outcome feedback may be mitigated by constructive framing, creating a more nuanced model of feedback efficacy. This moves beyond examining each dimension in isolation to map the conditions under which different feedback types succeed or fail.

ideas.title: Decoding Pragmatic Frames: How Children's Interpretation of Praise Mimics Language Pragmatics
ideas.description: This research seed expands by drawing an analogy from linguistic pragmatics, the study of how context and tone shape the interpretation of speech acts. The analogous problem is determining how listeners discern the intended meaning (illocutionary force) of an utterance beyond its literal words. That field's solution involves systematically manipulating paralinguistic cues and situational context in controlled scenarios. Adapted to developmental psychology, the study would investigate whether children misattribute process praise as person praise based on tone or context by creating experimental vignettes. Identical process-praise statements would be delivered with varying tones (warm/encouraging vs. flat/evaluative) and embedded in different narrative contexts (highlighting effort vs. inherent ease). The key adaptation is to measure children's explicit interpretations of the praise's intended meaning, treating the feedback as a pragmatic signal to be decoded, thereby directly testing how these extralinguistic factors alter the praise's perceived message and its subsequent motivational effect.

ideas.title: Neural and behavioral dynamics of group-level contingent self-worth from collective feedback
ideas.description: This research investigates how praising or criticizing a team's inherent traits versus its collaborative process shapes the group's collective resilience after failure, by simultaneously measuring the neural correlates of contingent self-worth in all team members. Using hyperscanning fMRI or multi-brain EEG, the study will capture how different types of group feedback create synchrony or divergence in brain networks associated with self-evaluation, threat, and social cohesion. This approach directly connects the neurobiological instantiation of feedback at the individual level to emergent, observable group behaviors like collective efficacy and task persistence, uncovering the mechanistic bridge between social feedback and team resilience.

ideas.title: Adapting Deliberate Practice Frameworks from Music Pedagogy to Train Process-Focused Feedback
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from music pedagogy, where teaching effective feedback is a solved problem through deliberate practice frameworks. In expert music instruction, teachers train students by breaking performances into technical components and giving feedback on the specific physical and cognitive processes, not the overall outcome. Adapting this solution, we propose to develop and test a program that trains adults to give process-focused feedback by teaching them to deconstruct tasks into core procedural steps, identify observable markers of effort or strategy, and deliver precise, corrective feedback on those modifiable processes. The intervention would adapt pedagogical tools like slowed-down scenario analysis, structured feedback rubrics focused on technique, and deliberate practice in identifying and commenting on process elements, moving beyond abstract advice to build a concrete, observable skill set.

ideas.title: Signal Fidelity in Developmental Feedback: A Communication Theory Approach to Praise Interpretation
ideas.description: This study challenges the assumption that children interpret praise as the giver intends by applying an analogical framework from digital communication theory. The analogous problem—a receiver misinterpreting a transmitted signal due to channel noise—is solved by modeling the communication system to quantify distortion and error rates. Adapted to developmental psychology, the research conceptualizes praise delivery as a communication channel: the giver's intent is the encoded signal, the child's predispositions (e.g., mindset, attachment history, cultural background) and immediate context are channel noise/filters, and the child's interpretation is the decoded output. Using experimental methods that vary praise type (signal) and assess child characteristics (channel properties), the study aims to develop a predictive model of interpretation fidelity, identifying which factors most significantly distort or clarify the intended message of praise.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Neuro-Behavioral Tracking of Praise-Type Effects on Post-Failure Resilience
ideas.description: This research integrates neuroimaging with longitudinal ecological and behavioral measures to overcome the snapshot limitation of the original idea. First, participants undergo fMRI while processing intelligence versus effort praise after a controlled failure. Crucially, this neural data is then used as a predictor in a multi-week ecological momentary assessment (EMA) phase, where participants report on real-world challenge encounters, persistence, and affect via a smartphone app. Finally, a follow-up lab session assesses behavioral persistence on novel difficult tasks. This design directly tests whether the brain's initial reactivity to a praise type predicts meaningful, long-term differences in resilience and motivational patterns.

ideas.title: Ecological Momentary Assessment of Cross-Cultural Person Praise Effects on Daily Coping and Identity
ideas.description: This research uses smartphone-based Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) to test the cross-cultural effects of person praise in real-world settings. Participants from both individualist and collectivist cultures will receive prompted surveys multiple times per day, capturing instances of receiving praise, its perceived type (person vs. process), and immediate cognitive, emotional, and behavioral coping responses. This design allows for the examination of how cultural values (e.g., group identity salience) moderate the real-time impact of praise on factors like motivation, stress, and self-concept within natural daily interactions, providing ecologically valid, process-oriented data.

ideas.title: Ecological Momentary Assessment of Manager Feedback: Naturalistic Observation of Person/Process/Outcome Styles in the Workplace
ideas.description: This research program uses naturalistic observation and Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) techniques to study how managers spontaneously deliver praise and criticism to employees in real workplace settings. Interactions are coded using the person/process/outcome framework to categorize feedback content as it naturally occurs. By linking these observed, momentary feedback styles to employees' concurrently reported motivation, affect, and perceived competence, the study validates and extends lab-based theories of feedback into the complex ecology of organizational behavior. This approach reveals the contextual triggers and immediate consequences of different feedback styles as they unfold in situ, providing high-ecological-validity evidence for leadership development.

ideas.title: Ecological Momentary Assessment of Stress Physiology in Response to Naturalistic Feedback
ideas.description: This research employs Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) to capture real-time cortisol samples and heart rate variability from children or adolescents immediately before and after naturally occurring episodes of praise and criticism from parents and teachers in home and classroom settings. By synchronizing precise behavioral coding of the feedback's content, tone, and context with these physiological measures, the study maps how specific feedback styles (e.g., process vs. person criticism) dynamically regulate the stress axis in real-world environments. This approach reveals not just acute reactivity but also how the accumulation of daily feedback interactions contributes to diurnal cortisol rhythms and autonomic nervous system regulation, offering a powerful lens on the biological embedding of social feedback.

ideas.title: Praise content matters more than delivery context

ideas.title: Quantifying Group-Level Praise Effects on Collective Risk-Taking and Resilience Using Behavioral Games
ideas.description: This research fuses behavioral economics games with group-level praise interventions to create a rigorous experimental paradigm. Teams receive either trait-focused or process-focused praise, then engage in collective investment or risk-taking games to quantify the 'cost' of praise in terms of altered group risk preferences and resource allocation. Following an induced collective failure in the game, the paradigm measures group resilience by observing changes in subsequent strategic coordination and investment recovery, providing behavioral and economic metrics for how praise types differentially impact team dynamics and performance.

ideas.title: A Process-Focused, Multi-Method Assessment of Implicit State Self-Worth Dynamics
ideas.description: This redesigned approach moves beyond a single IAT snapshot to capture the dynamic, implicit processes underlying contingent self-worth. It employs repeated, brief implicit behavioral tasks sensitive to momentary changes, synchronized with real-time psychophysiology, to triangulate the implicit affective and cognitive impact of evaluative feedback. Computational modeling of response time data further deconstructs whether feedback alters automatic associations or the ability to regulate them. This system provides a robust, process-oriented measure of implicit state self-worth that is temporally precise and methodologically distinct from explicit reports.

ideas.title: Adapting Psychophysics Signal Detection to Quantify Praise-Induced Performance Distortion
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core methodology of Signal Detection Theory from psychophysics to behavioral economics. The analogous problem in psychophysics is separating true perceptual ability from decision bias in an observer's responses. This is solved by designing controlled discrimination tasks that yield metrics for sensitivity (d') and response criterion. Adapted to the research on praise effects, we would create a performance task with inherent ambiguity (e.g., judging which of two nearly identical graphs shows a stronger trend). Participants perform the task under control conditions and after receiving specific praise (e.g., for accuracy or boldness). By applying SDT analysis, we can precisely quantify the 'cost' of praise not as a simple change in overall score, but as a shift in the decision criterion—measuring how much praise makes individuals more liberal or conservative in their judgments, independent of their actual discriminatory ability. This isolates the distortion introduced by the performance goal set by the praise.

ideas.title: Deconstructing Feedback Harm: A Dimensional Analysis in Competitive Environments
ideas.description: This research precisely isolates which characteristics of performance feedback cause harm in high-stakes competition, moving beyond vague categories like 'outcome-focused.' Through a multi-study design, it manipulates orthogonal feedback dimensions—such as attribution target, controllability, and temporal focus—within controlled competitive simulations. This allows for causal identification of the specific combinations that degrade subsequent performance, increase unethical behavior, or heighten stress. The result is a predictive model of feedback harm that can inform targeted interventions and training protocols for coaches, managers, and educators.

ideas.title: Signal Strength vs. Broadcast Medium: Isolating the Informational Value of Praise
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that the public vs. private context of praise delivery is its primary impactful component. Drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology's study of animal communication, where the informational content of a signal (an honest indicator of fitness) is analyzed separately from its broadcast medium, this research adapts that solution. It proposes experiments to isolate whether the motivational impact of praise on goals and persistence is rooted in its intrinsic informational 'signal' (e.g., specificity, attribution to controllable factors like effort) rather than the social 'broadcast' context (public or private). By systematically varying content quality and delivery context, the study aims to determine if context merely modulates the reception strength of the core, content-based signal.

ideas.title: Linguistic Profiling of Natural Feedback to Predict Behavioral Economic Outcomes in Children
ideas.description: This research integrates natural language processing (NLP) of parent-child speech transcripts with behavioral economic games to establish predictive, individual-difference links between natural language and economic behavior. First, NLP models will automatically code the valence, type, and linguistic structure of praise and criticism in large-scale, real-world conversational datasets. Subsequently, children from these recorded interactions will participate in tailored investment or risk-taking games that quantify the 'cost' of goals shaped by such feedback. The core innovation is using the linguistic profiles derived from natural speech to predict each child's precise behavioral parameters in the economic games, moving beyond correlation to model how specific communicative patterns mechanistically shape economic decision-making.

ideas.title: Developing and validating a real-time, NLP-powered feedback coach for process-focused communication
ideas.description: This research develops a digital intervention platform that uses real-time NLP to analyze a user's attempts at giving process praise or criticism during simulated or recorded interactions. The system then provides immediate, tailored feedback—highlighting linguistic markers of person vs. process focus, suggesting improved phrasing, and offering micro-practice exercises. This moves beyond static training programs by creating an adaptive coach that learns from large-scale linguistic data to personalize instruction, and beyond passive linguistic analysis by directly applying its insights to drive behavioral change in real-time communication.
